sentence: The following is a tutorial I wrote up that will give you an understanding about RS-232 and null modems, but you should still check your software!
label: comp.sys.ibm.pc.hardware

sentence: I will eplain a couple of details of RS-232.
label: comp.sys.ibm.pc.hardware

sentence: RS-232 is a commumications specification for communicating between a computer and a modem.
label: comp.sys.ibm.pc.hardware

sentence: Actually it can be between any end system and any communications hardware.
label: comp.sys.ibm.pc.hardware

sentence: The terminology used is Data Terminal Equipment (DTE) and DATA Communications Equipment (DCE).
label: comp.sys.ibm.pc.hardware

sentence: RS-232 spells out the voltage levels, the connector type, the pinouts, and the signal protocols.
label: comp.sys.ibm.pc.hardware

sentence: The connector is a "DB-25" but IBM has set an alternative "standard" of DB-9.
label: comp.sys.ibm.pc.hardware

sentence: The primary signals are Transmit Data (TD), Recieve Data (RD), and Signal Ground (SG).
label: comp.sys.ibm.pc.hardware

sentence: There are other signals that provide control between the DTE and the DCE.
label: comp.sys.ibm.pc.hardware

sentence: For example, the DTE announces that it is powered up and ready to participate in communications via the Data Terminal Ready (DTR) signal.
label: comp.sys.ibm.pc.hardware

sentence: DTR is an output on the DTE and an input on the DCE.
label: comp.sys.ibm.pc.hardware

sentence: Similarly, the DCE announces that it is ready to participate in communications via the Data Set Ready (DSR) signal.
label: comp.sys.ibm.pc.hardware

sentence: DSR is an input on the DTE and an output on the DCE.
label: comp.sys.ibm.pc.hardware

sentence: Ok that's five signals; there are only four more of interest.
label: comp.sys.ibm.pc.hardware

sentence: The Request To Send (RTS) signal is an output from the DTE (and an input to the DCE) that is used to ask the DCE permission to send data.
label: comp.sys.ibm.pc.hardware

sentence: If the DCE agrees, it sends an ok via the Clear To Send (CTS) signal.
label: comp.sys.ibm.pc.hardware

sentence: (For completeness, the CTS is an input to the DTE and an output from the DCE).
label: comp.sys.ibm.pc.hardware

sentence: The other two signals (of interest) are Ring Indicator (RI) and Data Carrier Detect (DCD).
label: comp.sys.ibm.pc.hardware

sentence: These are both inputs to the DTE and outputs from the DCE.
label: comp.sys.ibm.pc.hardware

sentence: RI is just what you would expect - a signal to the DTE saying that someone is attempting to establish a connection to the DCE.
label: comp.sys.ibm.pc.hardware

sentence: This is rather specific to the modem / telephone line setup.
label: comp.sys.ibm.pc.hardware

sentence: DCD is a way for the DCE to announce that the "connection" has been established i.e. the local DCE is talking to some remote DCE.
label: comp.sys.ibm.pc.hardware

sentence: There - that's it.
label: comp.sys.ibm.pc.hardware

sentence: Oh there are many other RS-232 signals defined, but they are obsolete.
label: comp.sys.ibm.pc.hardware

sentence: I have explained the nine signals that are on a PC.
label: comp.sys.ibm.pc.hardware

sentence: The pinouts are as follows: Name DB-9 DB-25 SG 5 7 TD 3 2 RD 2 3 DTR 4 20 DSR 6 6 DCD 1 8 RTS 7 4 CTS 8 5 RI 9 22 Now to address your problem at hand.
label: comp.sys.ibm.pc.hardware

sentence: When you connect a PC to a PC (a DTE to a DTE), there is no DCE pair in the middle and therefore the RS-232 signal definitions don't work out quite right.
label: comp.sys.ibm.pc.hardware

sentence: There is no DCE to assert the CTS, DSR, DCD, or RI.
label: comp.sys.ibm.pc.hardware

sentence: So the common thing to do is to not use these signals at all - and also forget about the DTR and RTS outputs as well.
label: comp.sys.ibm.pc.hardware

sentence: If this is done you simply make a null modem (a cable) that passes through SG, and crosses TD and RD (i.e. pin 2 of one end connects to pin 3 at the other end, etc).
label: comp.sys.ibm.pc.hardware

sentence: The problem with this solution is that a PC that wants to send data has no way of knowing if the other PC is ready.
label: comp.sys.ibm.pc.hardware

sentence: It would have to just send the data and hope it got through.
label: comp.sys.ibm.pc.hardware

sentence: Therefore a better null modem would include the DTR/DSR pair crossed.
label: comp.sys.ibm.pc.hardware

sentence: A particular point a confusion is in the software area.
label: comp.sys.ibm.pc.hardware

sentence: Just because you run the wires does not mean that the soft- ware will use them.
label: comp.sys.ibm.pc.hardware

sentence: If you are using the built-in BIOS to control the serial port, then the BIOS dictates what wires you need and how they are to be used.
label: comp.sys.ibm.pc.hardware

sentence: But if you have a comm.
label: comp.sys.ibm.pc.hardware

sentence: package such as Brooklyn Bridge, or Lap- Link or Telix, then those packages dictate the cabling requirements.
label: comp.sys.ibm.pc.hardware

sentence: The only thing you can guess reliably is the SG, TD, and RD.
label: comp.sys.ibm.pc.hardware

sentence: The DTR/DSR is also fairly common.
label: comp.sys.ibm.pc.hardware

sentence: There is one more issue that needs to be addressed here.
label: comp.sys.ibm.pc.hardware

sentence: And that is flow control.
label: comp.sys.ibm.pc.hardware

sentence: In the RS-232 scenerio, the DCE's are responsible for flow control and it is assumed that the DCE couldn't possible over-run the DTE.
label: comp.sys.ibm.pc.hardware

sentence: By this I mean that as long as the DTE has its DTR line asserted, the DCE will send data - without requesting permission first.
label: comp.sys.ibm.pc.hardware

sentence: Note the contrast to the other direction of data flow: the DTE must request permission by asserting the RTS line and it must wait for the CTS signal before it can send.
label: comp.sys.ibm.pc.hardware

sentence: And now in the DTE to DTE scenerio, the question is do you need flow control?
label: comp.sys.ibm.pc.hardware

sentence: The answser is usually.
label: comp.sys.ibm.pc.hardware

sentence: But how?
label: comp.sys.ibm.pc.hardware

sentence: For non-binary communications, one common technique is XON - XOFF which is implemented in software - no wires are dedicated to flow control; the TD and RD carry the XON and XOFF commands.
label: comp.sys.ibm.pc.hardware

sentence: This type of flow control can be used in addition to hardware flow control, but the problem is that you can't send binary data because your data might include the XON or XOFF codes and cause erroneous conrol signals.
label: comp.sys.ibm.pc.hardware

sentence: (A work around is to have the software insert and extract "escape" codes, but the hardware flow control is prefered).
label: comp.sys.ibm.pc.hardware

sentence: The problem with hard- ware flow control is that there is no standard.
label: comp.sys.ibm.pc.hardware

sentence: There are lots of ways to do it and I bet they have all been tried.
label: comp.sys.ibm.pc.hardware

sentence: One cornerstone of all the methods is to use the RTS/CTS for one direction.
label: comp.sys.ibm.pc.hardware

sentence: But beyond that anything is fair game.
label: comp.sys.ibm.pc.hardware

sentence: Again, you must look to you comm package for cabling requirements.
label: comp.sys.ibm.pc.hardware

sentence: But I bet you'll find that one cable (if wired properly) could work with all of the packages as long as they are of the same vintage.
label: comp.sys.ibm.pc.hardware

sentence: By the way, I am familiar with one package that uses RTS/CTS and DTR/DSR for flow control the RTS is request to send and the DTR is ready to recieve, the CTS is the clear to send and the DSR is the request to recieve.
label: comp.sys.ibm.pc.hardware

sentence: This does not mean a differnt cable - it is just software.
label: comp.sys.ibm.pc.hardware

sentence: The clincher to all of this cabling stuff is that it is common to have the UART directly controlled by the CTS signal which means that the CTS must be present even if you are not support- ing it in software.
label: comp.sys.ibm.pc.hardware

sentence: For these cases you will find cables that short the RTS to the CTS at the same end (or perhaps the DTR to the CTS).
label: comp.sys.ibm.pc.hardware

sentence: Other possible shorting might be done just because the software expects certain signals that you don't have (or don't feel like providing because it would mean additional wires).
label: comp.sys.ibm.pc.hardware

sentence: Specifically I am talking about the DSR.
label: comp.sys.ibm.pc.hardware

sentence: Shorting the DTR to the DSR is not a good idea in general but you may find cables like this.
label: comp.sys.ibm.pc.hardware

sentence: It is a bad idea because it defeats the whole purpose.
label: comp.sys.ibm.pc.hardware

sentence: One more "gotcha" signal is the DCD.
label: comp.sys.ibm.pc.hardware

sentence: Some software (including the PC BIOS) expects to see the DCD before it will work.
label: comp.sys.ibm.pc.hardware

sentence: This signal sometimes gets shorted with the DTR.
label: comp.sys.ibm.pc.hardware

sentence: But my favorite null modem has the RTS and CTS shorted at each end, but the RTS is also connected through to the other end and connected to DCD.
label: comp.sys.ibm.pc.hardware

sentence: And of course the DTR/DSR are crossed as are the TD/RD and of course the SG must pass through.
label: comp.sys.ibm.pc.hardware

sentence: This null modem works with most software.
label: comp.sys.ibm.pc.hardware

sentence: The flow control, if any, can use the RTS/DCD with the DTR/DSR.
label: comp.sys.ibm.pc.hardware

sentence: If your comm.
label: comp.sys.ibm.pc.hardware

sentence: package does not specify the cable required for PC to PC connection, then I suggest you use this one.

sentence: By initiating a DMA xfer.
label: comp.sys.ibm.pc.hardware

sentence: Seriously, busmastering adapter have their own DMA ability, they don't use the motherboards on-board DMA(which is *MUCH* slower).
label: comp.sys.ibm.pc.hardware

sentence: ISA has no bus arbitration, so if two busmastering cards in 1 ISA system try to do DMA xfers on the same DMA channel the system will lock or crash.(I forget)
label: comp.sys.ibm.pc.hardware

sentence: Their are 8 DMA channels in an ISA system. 0-7. 0-3 are 8-bit & 4-7 are 16-bit.
label: comp.sys.ibm.pc.hardware

sentence: The system uses DMA 0, a SoundBlaster uses DMA 1.
label: comp.sys.ibm.pc.hardware

sentence: I could buy a busmastering XGA-2 video card & a busmastering SCSI HA.
label: comp.sys.ibm.pc.hardware

sentence: In order for them to work properly, I would have to find out what DMA channel the XGA-2 card uses and then simply configure the SCSI HA to use a different DMA channel for its DMA xfers.
label: comp.sys.ibm.pc.hardware

sentence: I don't know if multiple DMA xfers can go on at the same time on ISA.
label: comp.sys.ibm.pc.hardware

sentence: I'm not sure if they can on EISA systems either.
label: comp.sys.ibm.pc.hardware

sentence: I do know that on EISA/MCA systems, you can allow BM cards to use the same DMA channel.

sentence: The alternative approach (to CISC), which has been adopted by many in recent years, has come to be known as "RISC": the Reduced Instruction Set Computer.
label: comp.os.ms-windows.misc

sentence: This can be characterised simply as "Simpler is Faster"; by simplifying the design (e.g. by reducing the variety of instructions & addressing modes), the hardware can be designed to run faster.
label: comp.os.ms-windows.misc

sentence: Even at the cost of needing more instructions, the same task can be done more quickly by the simpler, faster design.
label: comp.os.ms-windows.misc

sentence: A typical RISC processor will: o provide a large number of registers (e.g. 32); o perform all data operations on registers; o provide few addressing modes (e.g. immediate or 'register + offset'); o only allow load & store operations to access memory; o only use a few instruction formats; o only support a few data types (e.g. integer, unsigned, floating).
label: comp.os.ms-windows.misc

sentence: The problem is the LASTDRIVE command and the way NetWare in general (and in this case NETX in particular) adds drives to the device chain.
label: comp.os.ms-windows.misc

sentence: Setting LASTDRIVE=Z means there are no "unassigned" (as opposed to "unused") drive letters for NetWare to use, as it tacks its drive mappings -onto the end- of the existing list of drives.
label: comp.os.ms-windows.misc

sentence: W4WG obviously attaches its network drives to "existing, unused" drive letters.
label: comp.os.ms-windows.misc

sentence: I'd guess the next thing I'd try is something like LASTDRIVE=M, which on most machines will leave a fair swathe of drives for W4WG and still allow up to 13 NetWare drive mappings as well.

sentence: Here's a brief description of how DOS stores files: There are three pieces to a file.
label: comp.os.ms-windows.misc

sentence: The directory entry, the FAT chain, and the data area.
label: comp.os.ms-windows.misc

sentence: You can think of these as a sheet of lined notebook paper, a sheet of graph paper, and a stack of 3X5 cards.
label: comp.os.ms-windows.misc

sentence: The directory entry (notebook paper) holds the file name, actual size, and first cluster number.
label: comp.os.ms-windows.misc

sentence: It also holds some other information that's not important right now.
label: comp.os.ms-windows.misc

sentence: The File Allocation Table (FAT) chain (graph paper) tells where to find the actual data.
label: comp.os.ms-windows.misc

sentence: Each square of graph paper holds a number.
label: comp.os.ms-windows.misc

sentence: If the number is zero, the cluster associated with this box is available.
label: comp.os.ms-windows.misc

sentence: If it holds a "magic" number, it is either the last piece of a file or a bad (unuseable) spot on the disk.
label: comp.os.ms-windows.misc

sentence: Any other number tells which cluster contains the next section of the file.
label: comp.os.ms-windows.misc

sentence: The data area (3X5 cards) is where the actual information is stored.
label: comp.os.ms-windows.misc

sentence: The data area is organized as clusters of a fixed size; storage is doled out in "chunks" of one cluster each.
label: comp.os.ms-windows.misc

sentence: (In your case, one cluster is 2048 bytes.)
label: comp.os.ms-windows.misc

sentence: As a cluster is filled, another is allocated.
label: comp.os.ms-windows.misc

sentence: To read a file, you first look at the directory entry to get the starting cluster number.
label: comp.os.ms-windows.misc

sentence: Now you read the data from that cluster.
label: comp.os.ms-windows.misc

sentence: Next, look at the FAT entry for the cluster you just read.
label: comp.os.ms-windows.misc

sentence: This will tell you the cluster number for the next chunk of the file.
label: comp.os.ms-windows.misc

sentence: Naturally, these numbers are usually sequential, but they can jump around and even go backwards.
label: comp.os.ms-windows.misc

sentence: Continue reading, one cluster at a time, as you walk this chain through the FAT, until you hit the marker which says it's the last cluster in the file.
label: comp.os.ms-windows.misc

sentence: CHKDSK is the DOS utility that checks the sanity and coherence of the directories and the FAT and can bludgeon most flaws into submission.
label: comp.os.ms-windows.misc

sentence: It doesn't have any intelligence, so you have to double-check anything it "fixes".
label: comp.os.ms-windows.misc

sentence: Now let's do a bit of a post-mortem: All of these files have sizes (according to the FAT) which don't match the size reported in their directory entries.
label: comp.os.ms-windows.misc

sentence: CHKDSK /F will alter the directory entries to match the FAT size.
label: comp.os.ms-windows.misc

sentence: (In other words, the directory entry for CVPIC.EXE may say the file is 64,877 bytes long.
label: comp.os.ms-windows.misc

sentence: But CHKDSK found a FAT chain of 43 clusters attached to it.
label: comp.os.ms-windows.misc

sentence: (My numbers, obviously, are made up.))
label: comp.os.ms-windows.misc

sentence: Disk space was found which is allocated in the FAT, but is not attached to any directory entry.
label: comp.os.ms-windows.misc

sentence: CHKDSK /F gives you the option of converting these "lost chains" to files.
label: comp.os.ms-windows.misc

sentence: You can then examine the files (FILE0000.CHK through FILE0223.CHK) and rename or discard them.
label: comp.os.ms-windows.misc

sentence: Or, if you tell CHKDSK _not_ to convert them to files, then those clusters will simply be marked "available" in the FAT. ...
label: comp.os.ms-windows.misc

sentence: These files are both referencing the _same_ data cluster.

sentence: PCI --- A company called PCI, Inc., out of Richmond Hill, Ontario, Canada, makes an array of software utilities for processing, manipulation, and use of remote sensing data in eight or ten different "industry standard" formats: LGSOWG, BSQ, LANDSAT, and a couple of others whose titles I forget.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: The software is available in versions for MS-DOS, Unix workstations (among them HP, Sun, and IBM), and VMS, and quite possibly other platforms by now.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: I use the VMS version.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: The "PCI software" consists of several classes/groups/packages of utilities, grouped by function but all operating on a common "PCI database" disk file.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: The "Tape I/O" package is a set of utility programs which read from the various remote-sensing industry tape formats INTO, or write those formats out FROM, the "PCI database" file; this is the only package I use or know much about.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: Other packages can display data from the PCI database to one or another of several PCI-supported third-party color displays, output numeric or bitmap representation of image data to an attached printer, e.g. an Epson-type dot-matrix graphics printer.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: You might be more spe- cifically interested in the mathematical operations package: histo-
label: comp.graphics

sentence: gram and Fourier analysis, equalization, user-specified operations (e.g. "multiply channel 1 by 3, add channel 2, and store as channel 5"), and God only knows what all else -- there's a LOT.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: I don't have and don't use these, so can't say much about them; you only buy the packages your particular application/interest calls for.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: Each utility is controlled by from one to eight "parameters," read from a common "parameter file" which must be (in VMS anyway) in your "default directory."
label: comp.graphics

sentence: Some utilities will share parameters and use the same parameter for a different purpose, so it can get a bit confusing setting up a series of operations.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: The standard PCI environment contains a scripting language very similar to IBM-PC BASIC, but which allows you to automate the process of setting up parameters for a common, complicated, lengthy or difficult series of utility executions.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: (In VMS I can also invoke utilities independently from a DCL command procedure.)
label: comp.graphics

sentence: There's also an optional programming library which allows you to write compiled language programs which can interface with (read from/write to)
label: comp.graphics

sentence: the PCI data structures (database file, parameter file).
label: comp.graphics

sentence: The PCI software is designed specifically for remote-sensing images, but requires such a level of operator expertise that, once you reach the level where you can handle r-s images, you can figure out ways to handle a few other things as well.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: DISIMP ------ (Device Independent Software for Image Processing) is a powerful system providing both user friendliness and high functionality in interactive times.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: Feature Description DISIMP incorporates a rich library of image processing utilities and spatial data options.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: All functions can be easily accessed via the DISIMP executive.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: This menu is modular in design and groups image processes by their function.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: Such a logical structure means that complicated processes are simply a progression through a series of modules.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: Processes include image rectification, classification (unsupervised and supervised), intensity transformations, three dimensional display and Principal Component Analysis.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: DISIMP also supports the more simple and effective enhancement techniques of filtering, band subtraction and ratioing.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: Host Configuration Requirements Running on UNIX workstations, DISIMP is capable of processing the more computational intensive techniques in interactive processing times.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: DISIMP is available in both Runtime and Programmer's environments.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: Using the Programmers environment, utilities can be developed for specific applications programs.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: Graphics are governed by an icon-based Display Panel which allows quick enhancments of a displayed image.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: Manipulations of Look Up Tables, colour stretches, changes to histograms, zooming and panning can be interactively driven through this control.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: A range of geographic projections enables DISIMP to integrate data of image, graphic and textual types.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: Images can be rectified by a number of coordinate systems, providing the true geographic knowledge essential for ground truthing.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: Overlays of grids, text and vector data can be added to further enhance referenced imagery.

sentence: Feature description HIPS is a set of image processing modules which together provide a powerful suite of tools for those interested in research, system development and teaching.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: It handles sequences of images (movies) in precisely the same manner as single frames.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: Programs and subroutines have been developed for simple image transformations, filtering, convolution, Fourier and other transform processing, edge detection and line drawing manipulation, digital image compression and transmission methods, noise generation, and image statistics computation.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: Over 150 such image transformation programs have been developed.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: As a result, almost any image processing task can be performed quickly and conveniently.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: Additionally, HIPS allows users to easily integrate their own custom routines.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: New users become effective using HIPS on their first day.

sentence: HIPS features images that are self-documenting.

sentence: Each image stored in the system contains a history of the transformations that have been applied to that image.

sentence: HIPS includes a small set of subroutines which primarily deals with a standardized image sequence header, and a large library of image transformation tools in the form of UNIX ``filters''.

sentence: It comes complete with source code, on-line manual pages, and on-line documentation.

sentence: ---this file last updated..4-21-93--- WHAT IS OTIS?
label: comp.graphics

sentence: OTIS is here for the purpose of distributing original artwork and photographs over the network for public perusal, scrutiny, and distribution.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: Digital immortality.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: The basic idea behind "digital immortality" is that computer networks are here to stay and that anything interesting you deposit on them will be around near-forever.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: The GIFs and JPGs of today will be the artifacts of a digital future.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: Perhaps they'll be put in different formats, perhaps only surviving on backup tapes....
label: comp.graphics

sentence: but they'll be there...and someone will dig them up.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: If that doesn't interest you...
label: comp.graphics

sentence: OTIS also offers a forum for critique and exhibition of your works....a virtual art gallery that never closes and exists in an information dimension where your submissions will hang as wallpaper on thousands of glowing monitors.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: Suddenly, life is breathed into your work...and by merit of it's stimulus, it will travel the globe on pulses of light and electrons.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: Spectators are welcome also, feel free to browse the gallery and let the artists know what you think of their efforts.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: Keep your own copies of the images to look at when you've got the gumption... that's what they're here for.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: OTIS currently (as of 4/21/93) has two FTP sites.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: 141.214.4.135 (projects/otis), the UWI site sunsite.unc.edu (/pub/multimedia/pictures/OTIS), the SUNsite (you can also GOPHER to this site for OTIS as well)
label: comp.graphics

sentence: Merely "anonymous FTP" to either site on Internet and change to the appropriate directory.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: Don't forget to get busy and use the "bin" command to make sure you're in binary.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: OTIS has also been spreading to some dial-up BBS systems around North America....the following systems have a substancial supply of OTIStuff...
label: comp.graphics

sentence: Underground Cafe (Omaha) (402.339.0179) 2 lines CyberDen (SanFran?)
label: comp.graphics

sentence: HOW DO YOU CONTRIBUTE?
label: comp.graphics

sentence: What happens is...you draw a pretty picture or take a lovely photo, get it scanned into an image file, then either FTP-put it in the CONTRIB/Incoming directory or use UUENCODE to send it to me (email addresses at eof) in email.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: After the image is received, it will be put into the correct directory.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: Computer originated works are also welcome.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: OTIS' directories house two types of image files, GIF and JPG.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: GIF and JPG files require, oddly enough, a GIF or JPG viewer to see.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: These viewers are available for all types of computers at most large FTP sites around Internet.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: JPG viewers are a bit tougher to find.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: If you can't find one, but do have a GIF viewer, you can obtain a JPG-to-GIF conversion program which will change JPG files to a standard GIF format.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: OTIS also accepts animation files.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: When you submit image files, please send me email at the same time stating information about what you uploaded and whether it is to be used (in publications or other projects) or if it is merely for people to view.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: Also, include some biographical information on yourself, we'll be having info-files on each contributing artist and their works.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: You can also just upload a text-file of info about yourself (instead of emailing).
label: comp.graphics

sentence: If you have pictures, but no scanner, there is hope.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: Merely send copies to: The OTIS Project c/o Ed Stastny PO BX 241113 Omaha, NE 68124-1113 I will either scan them myself or get them to someone who will scan them.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: Include an ample SASE if you want your stuff back.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: Also include information on each image, preferably a 1-3 line description of the image that we can include in the infofile in the directory where it's finally put.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: If you have preferences as to what the images are to be named, include those as well.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: Conversely, if you have a scanner and would like to help out, please contact me and we'll arrange things.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: If you want to submit your works by disk, peachy.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: Merely send a 3.5" disk to the above address (Omaha) and a SASE if you want your disk back.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: This is good for people who don't have direct access to encoders or FTP, but do have access to a scanner.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: We accept disks in either Mac or IBM compatible format.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: If possible, please submit image files as GIF or JPG.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: If you can't...we can convert from most formats...
label: comp.graphics

sentence: we'd just rather not have to.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: At senders request, we can also fill disks with as much OTIS as they can stand.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: Even if you don't have stuff to contribute, you can send a blank disk and an SASE (or $2.50 for disk, postage and packing) to get a slab-o-OTIS.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: As of 04/21/93, we're at about 18 megabytes of files, and growing.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: Email me for current archive size and directory.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: The images distributed by the OTIS project may be distributed freely on the condition that the original filename is kept and that it is not altered in any way (save to convert from one image format to another).
label: comp.graphics

sentence: In fact, we encourage files to be distributed to local bulletin boards and such.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: If you could, please transport the appropriate text files along with the images.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: It would also be nice if you'd send me a note when you did post images from OTIS to your local bbs.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: I just want to keep track of them so participants can have some idea how widespread their stuff is.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: It's the purpose of OTIS to get these images spread out as much as possible.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: If you have the time, please upload a few to your favorite BBS system....or even just post this "info-file" there.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: It would be keen of you.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: If you want to use any of the works you find on the OTIS directory, you'll have to check to see if permission has been granted and the stipulations of the permission (such as free copy of publication, or full address credit).
label: comp.graphics

sentence: You will either find this in the ".rm" file for the image or series of images...or in the "Artists" directory under the Artists name.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: If permission isn't explicitly given, then you'll have to contact the artist to ask for it.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: If no info is available, email me (ed@cwis.unomaha.edu), and I'll get in contact with the artist for you, or give you their contact information.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: When you DO use permitted work, it's always courteous to let the artist know about it, perhaps even send them a free copy or some such compensation for their files.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: NAMING IMAGES?
label: comp.graphics

sentence: Please keep the names of your files in "dos" format.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: That means, keep the filename (before .jpg or .gif) to eight characters or less.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: The way I usually do it is to use the initials of the artist, plus a three or four digit "code" for the series of images, plus the series number.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: Thus, Leonardo DeVinci's fifth mechanical drawing would be something like: ldmek5.gif
label: comp.graphics

sentence: OR ldmek5.jpg OR ldmech5.gif ETC Keeping the names under 8 characters assures that the filename will remain intact on all systems.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: When creating image files, be sure to at least include your name somewhere on or below the picture.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: This gives people a reference in case they'd like to contact you.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: You may also want to include a title, address or other information you'd like people to know.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: That's about it for now.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: More "guidelines" will be added as needed.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: Your input is expected.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: The OTIS Project has no connection to the Church of OTIS (a sumerian deity) or it's followers, be they pope, priest, or ezine administrator.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: We do take sacrifices and donations however.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: The OTIS Project is here for the distribution of original image files.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: The files will go to the public at large.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: It's possible, as with any form of mass-media, that someone could unscrupulously use your images for financial gain.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: Unless you've given permission for that, it's illegal.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: OTIS takes no responsibility for this.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: In simple terms, all rights revert to the author/artist.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: To leave an image on OTIS is to give permission for it to be viewed, copied and distributed electronically.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: If you don't want your images distributed all-over, don't upload them.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: To leave an image on OTIS is NOT giving permission to have it used in any publication or broadcast that incurs profit (this includes, but is not limited to, magazines, newsletters, clip-art software, screen-printed clothing, etc).
label: comp.graphics

sentence: You must give specific permission for this sort of usage.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: Remember, the operative term is "stimulate".

sentence: [1] What is JPEG?
label: comp.graphics

sentence: JPEG (pronounced "jay-peg") is a standardized image compression mechanism.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: JPEG stands for Joint Photographic Experts Group, the original name of the committee that wrote the standard.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: JPEG is designed for compressing either full-color or gray-scale digital images of "natural", real-world scenes.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: It does not work so well on non-realistic images, such as cartoons or line drawings.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: JPEG does not handle black-and-white (1-bit-per-pixel) images, nor does it handle motion picture compression.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: Standards for compressing those types of images are being worked on by other committees, named JBIG and MPEG respectively.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: JPEG is "lossy", meaning that the image you get out of decompression isn't quite identical to what you originally put in.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: The algorithm achieves much of its compression by exploiting known limitations of the human eye, notably the fact that small color details aren't perceived as well as small details of light-and-dark.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: Thus, JPEG is intended for compressing images that will be looked at by humans.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: If you plan to machine-analyze your images, the small errors introduced by JPEG may be a problem for you, even if they are invisible to the eye.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: A useful property of JPEG is that the degree of lossiness can be varied by adjusting compression parameters.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: This means that the image maker can trade off file size against output image quality.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: You can make *extremely* small files if you don't mind poor quality; this is useful for indexing image archives, making thumbnail views or icons, etc. etc.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: Conversely, if you aren't happy with the output quality at the default compression setting, you can jack up the quality until you are satisfied, and accept lesser compression.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: [2] Why use JPEG?
label: comp.graphics

sentence: There are two good reasons: to make your image files smaller, and to store 24-bit-per-pixel color data instead of 8-bit-per-pixel data.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: Making image files smaller is a big win for transmitting files across networks and for archiving libraries of images.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: Being able to compress a 2 Mbyte full-color file down to 100 Kbytes or so makes a big difference in disk space and transmission time!
label: comp.graphics

sentence: (If you are comparing GIF and JPEG, the size ratio is more like four to one.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: More details below.)
label: comp.graphics

sentence: If your viewing software doesn't support JPEG directly, you'll have to convert JPEG to some other format for viewing or manipulating images.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: Even with a JPEG-capable viewer, it takes longer to decode and view a JPEG image than to view an image of a simpler format (GIF, for instance).
label: comp.graphics

sentence: Thus, using JPEG is essentially a time/space tradeoff: you give up some time in order to store or transmit an image more cheaply.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: It's worth noting that when network or phone transmission is involved, the time savings from transferring a shorter file can be much greater than the extra time to decompress the file.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: I'll let you do the arithmetic yourself.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: The other reason why JPEG will gradually replace GIF as a standard Usenet posting format is that JPEG can store full color information: 24 bits/pixel (16 million colors) instead of 8 or less (256 or fewer colors).
label: comp.graphics

sentence: If you have only 8-bit display hardware then this may not seem like much of an advantage to you.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: Within a couple of years, though, 8-bit GIF will look as obsolete as black-and-white MacPaint format does today.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: Furthermore, for reasons detailed in section 7, JPEG is far more useful than GIF for exchanging images among people with widely varying color display hardware.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: Hence JPEG is considerably more appropriate than GIF for use as a Usenet posting standard.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: [3] When should I use JPEG, and when should I stick with GIF?
label: comp.graphics

sentence: JPEG is *not* going to displace GIF entirely; for some types of images, GIF is superior in image quality, file size, or both.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: One of the first things to learn about JPEG is which kinds of images to apply it to.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: As a rule of thumb, JPEG is superior to GIF for storing full-color or gray-scale images of "realistic" scenes; that means scanned photographs and similar material.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: JPEG is superior even if you don't have 24-bit display hardware, and it is a LOT superior if you do.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: (See section 7 for details.)
label: comp.graphics

sentence: GIF does significantly better on images with only a few distinct colors, such as cartoons and line drawings.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: In particular, large areas of pixels that are all *exactly* the same color are compressed very efficiently indeed by GIF.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: JPEG can't squeeze these files as much as GIF does without introducing visible defects.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: This sort of image is best kept in GIF form.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: (In particular, single-color borders are quite cheap in GIF files, but they should be avoided in JPEG files.)
label: comp.graphics

sentence: JPEG also has a hard time with very sharp edges: a row of pure-black pixels adjacent to a row of pure-white pixels, for example.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: Sharp edges tend to come out blurred unless you use a very high quality setting.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: Again, this sort of thing is not found in scanned photographs, but it shows up fairly often in GIF files: borders, overlaid text, etc.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: The blurriness is particularly objectionable with text that's only a few pixels high.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: If you have a GIF with a lot of small-size overlaid text, don't JPEG it.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: Computer-drawn images (ray-traced scenes, for instance) usually fall between scanned images and cartoons in terms of complexity.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: The more complex and subtly rendered the image, the more likely that JPEG will do well on it.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: The same goes for semi-realistic artwork (fantasy drawings and such).
label: comp.graphics

sentence: Plain black-and-white (two level) images should never be converted to JPEG.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: You need at least about 16 gray levels before JPEG is useful for gray-scale images.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: It should also be noted that GIF is lossless for gray-scale images of up to 256 levels, while JPEG is not.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: If you have an existing library of GIF images, you may wonder whether you should convert them to JPEG.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: You will lose a little image quality if you do.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: (Section 7, which argues that JPEG image quality is superior to GIF, only applies if both formats start from a full-color original.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: If you start from a GIF, you've already irretrievably lost a great deal of information; JPEG can only make things worse.)
label: comp.graphics

sentence: However, the disk space savings may justify converting anyway.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: This is a decision you'll have to make for yourself.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: If you do convert a GIF library to JPEG, see section 14 for hints.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: Be prepared to leave some images in GIF format, since some GIFs will not convert well.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: [4] How well does JPEG compress images?
label: comp.graphics

sentence: Pretty darn well.
label: comp.graphics

label: comp.graphics

label: comp.graphics

sentence: In this case JPEG can make a file that's a factor of four or five smaller than a GIF of comparable quality (the -Q 75 file is every bit as good as the GIF, better if you have a full-color display).
label: comp.graphics

sentence: This seems to be a typical ratio for real-world scenes.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: [5] What are good "quality" settings for JPEG?
label: comp.graphics

sentence: Most JPEG compressors let you pick a file size vs. image quality tradeoff by selecting a quality setting.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: There seems to be widespread confusion about the meaning of these settings.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: "Quality 95" does NOT mean "keep 95% of the information", as some have claimed.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: The quality scale is purely arbitrary; it's not a percentage of anything.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: The name of the game in using JPEG is to pick the lowest quality setting (smallest file size) that decompresses into an image indistinguishable from the original.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: This setting will vary from one image to another and from one observer to another, but here are some rules of thumb.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: The default quality setting (-Q 75) is very often the best choice.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: This setting is about the lowest you can go without expecting to see defects in a typical image.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: Try -Q 75 first; if you see defects, then go up.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: Except for experimental purposes, never go above -Q 95; saying -Q 100 will produce a file two or three times as large as -Q 95, but of hardly any better quality.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: If the image was less than perfect quality to begin with, you might be able to go down to -Q 50 without objectionable degradation.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: On the other hand, you might need to go to a HIGHER quality setting to avoid further degradation.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: The second case seems to apply much of the time when converting GIFs to JPEG.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: The default -Q 75 is about right for compressing 24-bit images, but -Q 85 to 95 is usually better for converting GIFs (see section 14 for more info).
label: comp.graphics

sentence: If you want a very small file (say for preview or indexing purposes) and are prepared to tolerate large defects, a -Q setting in the range of 5 to 10 is about right.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: -Q 2 or so may be amusing as "op art".
label: comp.graphics

sentence: (Note: the quality settings discussed in this article apply to the free JPEG software described in section 6B, and to many programs based on it.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: Other JPEG implementations, such as Image Alchemy, may use a completely different quality scale.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: Some programs don't even provide a numeric scale, just "high"/"medium"/"low"-style choices.)
label: comp.graphics

sentence: X Windows: XV (shareware, $25) is an excellent viewer for JPEG, GIF, and many other image formats.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: It can also do format conversion and some simple image manipulations.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: Z. Version 3.00 is a major upgrade with support for 24-bit displays and many other improvements; however, it is brand new and still has some bugs lurking.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: If you prefer not to be on the bleeding edge, stick with version 2.21, also available from export.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: Note that version 2.21 is not a good choice if you have a 24-bit display (you'll get only 8-bit color), nor for converting 24-bit images to JPEG.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: But 2.21 works fine for converting GIF and other 8-bit images to JPEG.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: CAUTION: there is a glitch in version 2.21: be sure to check the "save at normal size" checkbox when saving a JPEG file, or the file will be blurry.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: Another good choice for X Windows is John Cristy's free ImageMagick package, also available from export.lcs.mit.edu, file contrib/ImageMagick.tar.Z.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: This package handles many image processing and conversion tasks.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: The ImageMagick viewer handles 24-bit displays correctly; for colormapped displays, it does better (though slower) color quantization than XV or the basic free JPEG software.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: Both of the above are large, complex packages.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: If you just want a simple image viewer, try xloadimage or xli.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: xloadimage supports JPEG in its latest release, 3.03.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: xloadimage is free and available from export.lcs.mit.edu, file contrib/xloadimage-3.03.tar.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: Z. xli is a variant version of xloadimage, said by its fans to be somewhat faster and more robust than the original.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: (The current xli is indeed faster and more robust than the current xloadimage, at least with respect to JPEG files, because it has the IJG v4 decoder while xloadimage 3.03 is using a hacked-over v1.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: The next xloadimage release will fix this.)
label: comp.graphics

sentence: xli is also free and available from export.lcs.mit.edu, file contrib/xli.1.14.tar.

sentence: MS-DOS:
label: comp.graphics

sentence: This covers plain DOS; for Windows or OS/2 programs, see the next headings.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: One good choice is Eric Praetzel's free DVPEG, which views JPEG and GIF files.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: The current version, 2.5, is available by FTP from sunee.uwaterloo.ca (129.97.50.50), file pub/jpeg/viewers/dvpeg25.zip.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: This is a good basic viewer that works on either 286 or 386/486 machines.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: The user interface is not flashy, but it's functional.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: Another freeware JPEG/GIF/TGA viewer is Mohammad Rezaei's Hiview.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: The current version, 1.2, is available from Simtel20 and mirror sites (see NOTE below), file msdos/graphics/hv12.zip.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: Hiview requires a 386 or better CPU and a VCPI-compatible memory manager (QEMM386 and 386MAX work; Windows and OS/2 do not).
label: comp.graphics

sentence: Hiview is currently the fastest viewer for images that are no bigger than your screen.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: For larger images, it scales the image down to fit on the screen (rather than using panning/scrolling as most viewers do).
label: comp.graphics

sentence: You may or may not prefer this approach, but there's no denying that it slows down loading of large images considerably.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: Note: installation is a bit tricky; read the directions carefully!
label: comp.graphics

sentence: A shareware alternative is ColorView for DOS ($30).
label: comp.graphics

sentence: This is easier to install than either of the two freeware alternatives.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: Its user interface is also much spiffier-looking, although personally I find it harder to use --- more keystrokes, inconsistent behavior.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: It is faster than DVPEG but a little slower than Hiview, at least on my hardware.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: (For images larger than screen size, DVPEG and ColorView seem to be about the same speed, and both are faster than Hiview.)
label: comp.graphics

sentence: The current version is 2.1, available from Simtel20 and mirror sites (see NOTE below), file msdos/graphics/dcview21.zip.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: Requires a VESA graphics driver; if you don't have one, look in vesadrv2.zip or vesa-tsr.zip from the same directory.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: (Many recent PCs have a built-in VESA driver, so don't try to load a VESA driver unless ColorView complains that the driver is missing.)
label: comp.graphics

sentence: A second shareware alternative is Fullview, which has been kicking around the net for a while, but I don't know any stable archive location for it.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: The current (rather old) version is inferior to the above viewers anyway.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: The author tells me that a new version of Fullview will be out shortly and it will be submitted to the Simtel20 archives at that time.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: The well-known GIF viewer CompuShow (CSHOW) supports JPEG in its latest revision, 8.60a.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: However, CSHOW's JPEG implementation isn't very good: it's slow (about half the speed of the above viewers) and image quality is poor except on hi-color displays.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: Too bad ... it'd have been nice to see a good JPEG capability in CSHOW.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: Shareware, $25. Available from Simtel20 and mirror sites (see NOTE below), file msdos/gif/cshw860a.zip.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: Due to the remarkable variety of PC graphics hardware, any one of these viewers might not work on your particular machine.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: If you can't get *any* of them to work, you'll need to use one of the following conversion programs to convert JPEG to GIF, then view with your favorite GIF viewer.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: (If you have hi-color hardware, don't use GIF as the intermediate format; try to find a TARGA-capable viewer instead.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: VPIC5.0 is reputed to do the right thing with hi-color displays.)
label: comp.graphics

sentence: The Independent JPEG Group's free JPEG converters are FTPable from Simtel20 and mirror sites (see NOTE below), file msdos/graphics/jpeg4.zip (or jpeg4386.zip if you have a 386 and extended memory).
label: comp.graphics

sentence: These files are DOS compilations of the free source code described in section 6B; they will convert JPEG to and from GIF, Targa, and PPM formats.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: Handmade Software offers free JPEG<=>GIF conversion tools, GIF2JPG/JPG2GIF.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: These are slow and are limited to conversion to and from GIF format; in particular, you can't get 24-bit color output from a JPEG.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: The major advantage of these tools is that they will read and write HSI's proprietary JPEG format as well as the Usenet-standard JFIF format.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: Since HSI-format files are rather widespread on BBSes, this is a useful capability.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: Version 2.0 of these tools is free (prior versions were shareware).
label: comp.graphics

sentence: Get it from Simtel20 and mirror sites (see NOTE below), file msdos/graphics/gif2jpg2.zip.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: NOTE: do not use HSI format for files to be posted on Internet, since it is not readable on non-PC platforms.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: Handmade Software also has a shareware image conversion and manipulation package, Image Alchemy.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: This will translate JPEG files (both JFIF and HSI formats) to and from many other image formats.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: It can also display images.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: A demo version of Image Alchemy version 1.6.2 is available from Simtel20 and mirror sites (see NOTE below), file msdos/graphics/alch162.zip.

sentence: [7] What's all this hoopla about color quantization?
label: comp.graphics

sentence: Most people don't have full-color (24 bit per pixel) display hardware.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: Typical display hardware stores 8 or fewer bits per pixel, so it can display 256 or fewer distinct colors at a time.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: To display a full-color image, the computer must map the image into an appropriate set of representative colors.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: This process is called "color quantization".
label: comp.graphics

sentence: (This is something of a misnomer, "color selection" would be a better term.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: We're stuck with the standard usage though.)
label: comp.graphics

sentence: Clearly, color quantization is a lossy process.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: It turns out that for most images, the details of the color quantization algorithm have MUCH more impact on the final image quality than do any errors introduced by JPEG (except at the very lowest JPEG quality settings).
label: comp.graphics

sentence: Since JPEG is a full-color format, converting a color JPEG image for display on 8-bit-or-less hardware requires color quantization.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: This is true for *all* color JPEGs: even if you feed a 256-or-less-color GIF into JPEG, what comes out of the decompressor is *not* 256 colors, but thousands of colors.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: This happens because JPEG's lossiness affects each pixel a little differently, so two pixels that started with identical colors will probably come out with slightly different colors.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: Each original color gets "smeared" into a group of nearby colors.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: Therefore quantization is always required to display a color JPEG on a colormapped display, regardless of the image source.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: The only way to avoid quantization is to ask for gray-scale output.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: (Incidentally, because of this effect it's nearly meaningless to talk about the number of colors used by a JPEG image.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: Even if you attempted to count the number of distinct pixel values, different JPEG decoders would give you different results because of roundoff error differences.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: I occasionally see posted images described as "256-color JPEG".
label: comp.graphics

sentence: This tells me that the poster (a) hasn't read this FAQ and (b) probably converted the JPEG from a GIF.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: JPEGs can be classified as color or gray-scale (just like photographs), but number of colors just isn't a useful concept for JPEG.)
label: comp.graphics

sentence: On the other hand, a GIF image by definition has already been quantized to 256 or fewer colors.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: (A GIF *does* have a definite number of colors in its palette, and the format doesn't allow more than 256 palette entries.)
label: comp.graphics

sentence: For purposes of Usenet picture distribution, GIF has the advantage that the sender precomputes the color quantization, so recipients don't have to.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: This is also the *disadvantage* of GIF: you're stuck with the sender's quantization.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: If the sender quantized to a different number of colors than what you can display, you have to re-quantize, resulting in much poorer image quality than if you had quantized once from a full-color image.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: Furthermore, if the sender didn't use a high-quality color quantization algorithm, you're out of luck.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: For this reason, JPEG offers the promise of significantly better image quality for all users whose machines don't match the sender's display hardware.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: JPEG's full color image can be quantized to precisely match the user's display hardware.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: Furthermore, you will be able to take advantage of future improvements in quantization algorithms (there is a lot of active research in this area), or purchase better display hardware, to get a better view of JPEG images you already have.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: With a GIF, you're stuck forevermore with what was sent.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: It's also worth mentioning that many GIF-viewing programs include rather shoddy quantization routines.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: If you view a 256-color GIF on a 16-color EGA display, for example, you are probably getting a much worse image than you need to.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: This is partly an inevitable consequence of doing two color quantizations (one to create the GIF, one to display it), but often it's also due to sloppiness.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: JPEG conversion programs will be forced to use high quality quantizers in order to get acceptable results at all, and in normal use they will quantize directly to the number of colors to be displayed.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: Thus, JPEG is likely to provide better results than the average GIF program for low-color-resolution displays as well as high-resolution ones!
label: comp.graphics

sentence: Finally, an ever-growing number of people have better-than-8-bit display hardware already: 15-bit "hi-color" PC displays, true 24-bit displays on workstations and Macintoshes, etc.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: For these people, GIF is already obsolete, as it cannot represent an image to the full capabilities of their display.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: JPEG images can drive these displays much more effectively.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: Thus, JPEG is an all-around better choice than GIF for representing images in a machine-independent fashion.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: [8] How does JPEG work?
label: comp.graphics

sentence: The buzz-words to know are chrominance subsampling, discrete cosine transforms, coefficient quantization, and Huffman or arithmetic entropy coding.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: This article's long enough already, so I'm not going to say more than that here.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: For technical information, see the comp.compression FAQ.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: This is available from the news.answers archive at rtfm.mit.edu, in files /pub/usenet/news.answers/compression-faq/part[1-3].
label: comp.graphics

sentence: If you need help in using the news.answers archive, see the top of this article.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: [9] What about lossless JPEG?
label: comp.graphics

sentence: There's a great deal of confusion on this subject.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: The JPEG committee did define a truly lossless compression algorithm, i.e., one that guarantees the final output is bit-for-bit identical to the original input.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: However, this lossless mode has almost nothing in common with the regular, lossy JPEG algorithm, and it offers much less compression.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: At present, very few implementations of lossless JPEG exist, and all of them are commercial.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: Saying "-Q 100" to the free JPEG software DOES NOT get you a lossless image.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: What it does get rid of is deliberate information loss in the coefficient quantization step.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: There is still a good deal of information loss in the color subsampling step.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: (With the V4 free JPEG code, you can also say "-sample 1x1" to turn off subsampling.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: Keep in mind that many commercial JPEG implementations cannot cope with the resulting file.)
label: comp.graphics

sentence: Even with both quantization and subsampling turned off, the regular JPEG algorithm is not lossless, because it is subject to roundoff errors in various calculations.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: The maximum error is a few counts in any one pixel value; it's highly unlikely that this could be perceived by the human eye, but it might be a concern if you are doing machine processing of an image.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: At this minimum-loss setting, regular JPEG produces files that are perhaps half the size of an uncompressed 24-bit-per-pixel image.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: True lossless JPEG provides roughly the same amount of compression, but it guarantees bit-for-bit accuracy.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: If you have an application requiring lossless storage of images with less than 6 bits per pixel (per color component), you may want to look into the JBIG bilevel image compression standard.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: This performs better than JPEG lossless on such images.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: JPEG lossless is superior to JBIG on images with 6 or more bits per pixel; furthermore, JPEG is public domain (at least with a Huffman back end), while the JBIG techniques are heavily covered by patents.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: [10] Why all the argument about file formats?
label: comp.graphics

sentence: Strictly speaking, JPEG refers only to a family of compression algorithms; it does *not* refer to a specific image file format.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: The JPEG committee was prevented from defining a file format by turf wars within the international standards organizations.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: Since we can't actually exchange images with anyone else unless we agree on a common file format, this leaves us with a problem.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: In the absence of official standards, a number of JPEG program writers have just gone off to "do their own thing", and as a result their programs aren't compatible with anybody else's.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: The closest thing we have to a de-facto standard JPEG format is some work that's been coordinated by people at C-Cube Microsystems.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: They have defined two JPEG-based file formats: *
label: comp.graphics

sentence: JFIF (JPEG File Interchange Format), a "low-end" format that transports pixels and not much else.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: * TIFF/JPEG, aka TIFF 6.0, an extension of the Aldus TIFF format.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: TIFF is a "high-end" format that will let you record just about everything you ever wanted to know about an image, and a lot more besides :-).
label: comp.graphics

sentence: TIFF is a lot more complex than JFIF, and may well prove less transportable, because different vendors have historically implemented slightly different and incompatible subsets of TIFF.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: It's not likely that adding JPEG to the mix will do anything to improve this situation.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: Both of these formats were developed with input from all the major vendors of JPEG-related products; it's reasonably likely that future commercial products will adhere to one or both standards.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: I believe that Usenet should adopt JFIF as the replacement for GIF in picture postings.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: JFIF is simpler than TIFF and is available now; the TIFF 6.0 spec has only recently been officially adopted, and it is still unusably vague on some crucial details.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: Even when TIFF/JPEG is well defined, the JFIF format is likely to be a widely supported "lowest common denominator"; TIFF/JPEG files may never be as transportable.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: A particular case that people may be interested in is Apple's QuickTime software for the Macintosh.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: QuickTime uses a JFIF-compatible format wrapped inside the Mac-specific PICT structure.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: Conversion between JFIF and QuickTime JPEG is pretty straightforward, and several Mac programs are available to do it (see Mac portion of section 6A).
label: comp.graphics

sentence: If you have an editor that handles binary files, you can strip a QuickTime JPEG PICT down to JFIF by hand; see section 11 for details.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: Another particular case is Handmade Software's programs (GIF2JPG/JPG2GIF and Image Alchemy).
label: comp.graphics

sentence: These programs are capable of reading and writing JFIF format.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: By default, though, they write a proprietary format developed by HSI.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: This format is NOT readable by any non-HSI programs and should not be used for Usenet postings.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: Use the -j switch to get JFIF output.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: (This applies to old versions of these programs; the current releases emit JFIF format by default.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: You still should be careful not to post HSI-format files, unless you want to get flamed by people on non-PC platforms.)
label: comp.graphics

sentence: [11] How do I recognize which file format I have, and what do I do about it?
label: comp.graphics

sentence: If you have an alleged JPEG file that your software won't read, it's likely to be HSI format or some other proprietary JPEG-based format.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: You can tell what you have by inspecting the first few bytes of the file: 1.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: A JFIF-standard file will start with the characters (hex) FF D8 FF E0, followed by two variable bytes (often hex 00 10), followed by 'JFIF'.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: If you see FF D8 at the start, but not the rest of it, you may have a "raw JPEG" file.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: This is probably decodable as-is by JFIF software --- it's worth a try, anyway.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: 3. HSI files start with 'hsi1'.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: You're out of luck unless you have HSI software.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: Portions of the file may look like plain JPEG data, but they won't decompress properly with non-HSI programs.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: A Macintosh PICT file, if JPEG-compressed, will have a couple hundred bytes of header followed by a JFIF header (scan for 'JFIF').
label: comp.graphics

sentence: Strip off everything before the FF D8 and you should be able to read it.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: 5. Anything else: it's a proprietary format, or not JPEG at all.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: If you are lucky, the file may consist of a header and a raw JPEG data stream.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: If you can identify the start of the JPEG data stream (look for FF D8), try stripping off everything before that.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: In uuencoded Usenet postings, the characteristic JFIF pattern is "begin" line M_]C_X ...
label: comp.graphics

sentence: whereas uuencoded HSI files will start with "begin" line M:'-I ...
label: comp.graphics

sentence: If you learn to check for the former, you can save yourself the trouble of downloading non-JFIF files.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: [12] What about arithmetic coding?
label: comp.graphics

sentence: The JPEG spec defines two different "back end" modules for the final output of compressed data: either Huffman coding or arithmetic coding is allowed.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: The choice has no impact on image quality, but arithmetic coding usually produces a smaller compressed file.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: On typical images, arithmetic coding produces a file 5 or 10 percent smaller than Huffman coding.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: (All the file-size numbers previously cited are for Huffman coding.)
label: comp.graphics

sentence: Unfortunately, the particular variant of arithmetic coding specified by the JPEG standard is subject to patents owned by IBM, AT&T, and Mitsubishi.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: Thus *you cannot legally use arithmetic coding* unless you obtain licenses from these companies.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: (The "fair use" doctrine allows people to implement and test the algorithm, but actually storing any images with it is dubious at best.)
label: comp.graphics

sentence: At least in the short run, I recommend that people not worry about arithmetic coding; the space savings isn't great enough to justify the potential legal hassles.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: In particular, arithmetic coding *should not* be used for any images to be exchanged on Usenet.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: There is some small chance that the legal situation may change in the future.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: Stay tuned for further details.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: [13] Does loss accumulate with repeated compression/decompression?
label: comp.graphics

sentence: It would be nice if, having compressed an image with JPEG, you could decompress it, manipulate it (crop off a border, say), and recompress it without any further image degradation beyond what you lost initially.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: Unfortunately THIS IS NOT THE CASE.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: In general, recompressing an altered image loses more information, though usually not as much as was lost the first time around.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: The next best thing would be that if you decompress an image and recompress it *without changing it* then there is no further loss, i.e., you get an identical JPEG file.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: Even this is not true; at least, not with the current free JPEG software.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: It's essentially a problem of accumulation of roundoff error.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: If you repeatedly compress and decompress, the image will eventually degrade to where you can see visible changes from the first-generation output.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: (It usually takes many such cycles to get visible change.)
label: comp.graphics

sentence: One of the things on our to-do list is to see if accumulation of error can be avoided or limited, but I am not optimistic about it.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: In any case, the most that could possibly be guaranteed would be that compressing the unmodified full-color output of djpeg, at the original quality setting, would introduce no further loss.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: Even such simple changes as cropping off a border could cause further roundoff-error degradation.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: (If you're wondering why, it's because the pixel-block boundaries move.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: If you cropped off only multiples of 16 pixels, you might be safe, but that's a mighty limited capability!)
label: comp.graphics

sentence: The bottom line is that JPEG is a useful format for archival storage and transmission of images, but you don't want to use it as an intermediate format for sequences of image manipulation steps.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: Use a lossless format (PPM, RLE, TIFF, etc) while working on the image, then JPEG it when you are ready to file it away.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: Aside from avoiding degradation, you will save a lot of compression/decompression time this way :-).
label: comp.graphics

sentence: [14] What are some rules of thumb for converting GIF images to JPEG?
label: comp.graphics

sentence: As stated earlier, you *will* lose some amount of image information if you convert an existing GIF image to JPEG.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: If you can obtain the original full-color data the GIF was made from, it's far better to make a JPEG from that.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: But if you need to save space and have only the GIF to work from, here are some suggestions for getting maximum space savings with minimum loss of quality.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: The first rule when converting a GIF library is to look at each JPEG, to make sure you are happy with it, before throwing away the corresponding GIF; that will give you a chance to re-do the conversion with a higher quality setting if necessary.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: Some GIFs may be better left as GIFs, as explained in section 3; in particular, cartoon-type GIFs with sixteen or fewer colors don't convert well.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: You may find that a JPEG file of reasonable quality will be *larger* than the GIF.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: (So check the sizes too.)
label: comp.graphics

sentence: Experience to date suggests that large, high-visual-quality GIFs are the best candidates for conversion to JPEG.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: They chew up the most storage so offer the most potential savings, and they convert to JPEG with least degradation.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: Don't waste your time converting any GIF much under 100 Kbytes.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: Also, don't expect JPEG files converted from GIFs to be as small as those created directly from full-color originals.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: To maintain image quality you may have to let the converted files be as much as twice as big as straight-through JPEG files would be (i.e., shoot for 1/2 or 1/3rd the size of the GIF file, not 1/4th as suggested in earlier comparisons).
label: comp.graphics

sentence: Many people have developed an odd habit of putting a large constant-color border around a GIF image.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: While useless, this was nearly free in terms of storage cost in GIF files.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: It is NOT free in JPEG files, and the sharp border boundary can create visible artifacts ("ghost" edges).
label: comp.graphics

sentence: Do yourself a favor and crop off any border before JPEGing.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: (If you are on an X Windows system, XV's manual and automatic cropping functions are a very painless way to do this.)
label: comp.graphics

sentence: cjpeg's default Q setting of 75 is appropriate for full-color input, but for GIF inputs, Q settings of 85 to 95 often seem to be necessary to avoid image degradation.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: (If you apply smoothing as suggested below, the higher Q setting may not be necessary.)
label: comp.graphics

sentence: Color GIFs of photographs or complex artwork are usually "dithered" to fool your eye into seeing more than the 256 colors that GIF can actually store.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: If you enlarge the image, you will see that adjacent pixels are often of significantly different colors; at normal size the eye averages these pixels together to produce the illusion of an intermediate color value.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: The trouble with dithering is that, to JPEG, it looks like high-spatial-frequency color noise; and JPEG can't compress noise very well.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: The resulting JPEG file is both larger and of lower image quality than what you would have gotten from JPEGing the original full color image (if you had it).
label: comp.graphics

sentence: To get around this, you want to "smooth" the GIF image before compression.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: Smoothing averages together nearby pixels, thus approximating the color that you thought you saw anyway, and in the process getting rid of the rapid color changes that give JPEG trouble.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: Appropriate use of smoothing will often let you avoid using a high Q factor, thus further reducing the size of the compressed file, while still obtaining a better-looking output image than you'd get without smoothing.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: With the V4 free JPEG software (or products based on it), a simple smoothing capability is built in.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: Try "-smooth 10" or so when converting GIFs.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: Values of 10 to 25 seem to work well for high-quality GIFs.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: Heavy-handed dithering may require larger smoothing factors.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: (If you can see regular fine-scale patterns on the GIF image even without enlargement, then strong smoothing is definitely called for.)
label: comp.graphics

sentence: Too large a smoothing factor will blur the output image, which you don't want.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: If you are an image processing wizard, you can also do smoothing with a separate filtering program, such as pnmconvol from the PBMPLUS package.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: However, cjpeg's built-in smoother is a LOT faster than pnmconvol...
label: comp.graphics

sentence: The upshot of all this is that "cjpeg -quality 85 -smooth 10" is probably a good starting point for converting GIFs.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: But if you really care about the image, you'll want to check the results and maybe try a few other settings.
label: comp.graphics
