sentence: OK, with all the discussion about observed playback speeds with QuickTime, the effects of scaling and so on, I thought I'd do some more tests.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: First of all, I felt that my original speed test was perhaps less than realistic.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: The movie I had been using only had 18 frames in it (it was a version of the very first movie I created with the Compact Video compressor).
label: comp.graphics

sentence: I decided something a little longer would give closer to real-world results (for better or for worse).
label: comp.graphics

sentence: I pulled out a copy of "2001: A Space Odyssey" that I had recorded off TV a while back.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: About fifteen minutes into the movie, there's a sequence where the Earth shuttle is approaching the space station.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: Specifically, I digitized a portion of about 30 seconds' duration, zooming in on the rotating space station.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: I figured this would give a reasonable amount of movement between frames.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: To increase the differences between frames, I digitized it at only 5 frames per second, to give a total of 171 frames.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: I captured the raw footage at a resolution of 384*288 pixels with the Spigot card in my Centris 650 (quarter-size resolution from a PAL source).
label: comp.graphics

sentence: I then imported it into Premiere and put it through the Compact Video compressor, keeping the 5 fps frame rate.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: I created two versions of the movie: one scaled to 320*240 resolution, the other at 160*120 resolution.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: I used the default "2.00" quality setting in Premiere 2.0.1, and specified a key frame every ten frames.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: I then ran the 320*240 movie through the same "Raw Speed Test" program I used for the results I'd been reporting earlier.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: Result: a playback rate of over 45 frames per second.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: That's right, I was getting a much higher result than with that first short test movie.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: Just for fun, I copied the 320*240 movie to my external hard disk (a Quantum LP105S), and ran it from there.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: This time the playback rate was only about 35 frames per second.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: Obviously the 230MB internal hard disk (also a Quantum) is a significant contributor to the speed of playback.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: I modified my speed test program to allow the specification of optional scaling factors, and tried playing back the 160*120 movie scaled to 320*240 size.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: This time the playback speed was over 60 fps.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: Clearly, the poster who observed poor performance on scaled playback was seeing QuickTime 1.0 in action, not 1.5.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: I'd try my tests with QuickTime 1.0, but I don't think it's entirely compatible with my Centris and System 7.1...
label: comp.graphics

sentence: Unscaled, the playback rate for the 160*120 movie was over 100 fps.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: The other thing I tried was saving versions of the 320*240 movie with "preferred" playback rates greater than 1.0, and seeing how well they played from within MoviePlayer (ie with QuickTime's normal synchronized playback).
label: comp.graphics

sentence: A preferred rate of 9.0 (=> 45 fps) didn't work too well: the playback was very jerky.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: Compare this with the raw speed test, which achieved 45 fps with ease.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: I can't believe that QuickTime's synchronization code would add this much overhead: I think the slowdown was coming from the Mac system's task switching.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: A preferred rate of 7.0 (=> 35 fps) seemed to work fine: I couldn't see any evidence of stutter.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: I *think*
label: comp.graphics

sentence: I could see slight stutter, but with four key frames every second, it was hard to tell.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: I guess I could try recreating the movies with a longer interval between the key frames, to make the stutter more noticeable.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: Of course, this will also improve the compression slightly, which should speed up the playback performance even more...
label: comp.graphics

sentence: I'm asking for help on a sticky problem involving unreasonably low apparent precision in Z-buffering, that I've encountered in 2 different PEX implementations.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: I can't find any discussion of this problem in any resources I can lay hands on (e.g. the comp.windows.x.pex FAQ, Gaskins's _PEXlib_Programming_Manual_, vendors' documentation).
label: comp.graphics

sentence: I'm posting this article by itself on comp.graphics, and virtually the same article with a test program demonstrating the problem on comp.windows.x.pex.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: The problem is hard to describe without pictures, hence this article is longish.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: If you can run PEXlib 5.x programs and are interested, I encourage you to build and run the test program in comp.windows.x.pex to see the effect yourself and play with my approach to dealing with it.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: (It depends on the utility code from the above Gaskins book; instructions for fetching it via anonymous FTP are given.)
label: comp.graphics

sentence: The problem to be solved is to eliminate or minimize "stitching" artifacts resulting from the use of Z-buffering with polylines that are coplanar with filled areas.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: The interpolated Z values along a line will differ slightly, due to roundoff error, from the interpolated Z values across an area, even when the endpoints of the line are coincident with vertices of the area.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: Because of this, it's a tossup whether the Z-buffer will allow the line pixels or the area pixels to be displayed.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: Visually, the result tends to be a dashed-line effect even though the line is supposed to be solid.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: Using the PEXlib API, my approach to a solution is to use two slightly different PEX view mapping transforms, in two view table entries, one for the areas and one for the lines.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: The PEX structures or immediate- mode output must be organized so that one view table index is always in effect for areas, and the other is always in effect for lines.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: The result is a slight shift in NPC Z coordinates for the lines, so as to attempt to bias the tossup situations in favor of the lines.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: This shift is effected by moving the front and back clipping planes used in the PEXlib view table entry for lines just a hair "backwards" (i.e. smaller VRC Z coordinates), compared to their positions in the view table entry used for areas.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: This means that when a point is transformed to NPC, its Z value will be slightly bigger if it comes from a line than if it comes from an area, thus accomplishing the desired bias.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: I would expect the Z roundoff errors which cause the problem to amount to a few units at most, out of the entire dynamic range of the Z-buffer, typically from 0 to 65535 if not 16777215 (i.e. 16 or 24 bit Z-buffers).
label: comp.graphics

sentence: Therefore, it seems that a tiny fraction of the range of Z in VRC between the front and back clip planes ought to suffice to reliably fix the stitching.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: But in fact, experience shows that the shift has to be as much as 0.003 to 0.006 of the range.

sentence: Let's say you have a scanned image of a line drawing; in this case a boat, but it could be anything.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: On the drawing you have a set of reference points whose true x,y positions are known.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: Now you digitize the drawing manually (in this case, using Yaron Danon's excellent Digitize program).
label: comp.graphics

sentence: That is, you use a program which converts cursor positions to x,y and saves those values when you click the mouse.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: Upon digitizing you notice that the reference point values that come out of the digitizing process differ in small but significant ways from the known true values.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: This is understandable because the scanned drawing is a reproduction of the original and there are successive sources of distortion such as differential expansion and contraction of paper, errors introduced in the printing process, scanner errors and what have you.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: The errors are not uniform over the entire drawing, so "global" adjustments such as stretching/contracting uniformly over x or y, or rotating the whole drawing, are not satisfactory.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: So the question is: does any kind soul know of an algorithm for removing such distortion?
label: comp.graphics

sentence: In particular, if I have three sets of points Reference(x,y) (the known true values)
label: comp.graphics

sentence: DistortedReference(x,y) (the same points, with known errors) DistortedData(x,y) (other points, with unknown errors)
label: comp.graphics

sentence: what function of Reference and Distorted could I apply to DistortedData to remove the errors.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: I suspect the problem could be solved by treating the distorted reference points as resulting from the projection of a "bumpy" 3d surface, solving for the surface and then "flattening" it to remove the errors in the other data points.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: Any kind and informed soul out there have any ideas, or better yet, pointers to treatments of the same or similar problems?
label: comp.graphics

sentence: X-Received: from hydrox.enet; by decwrl.enet; Tue, 27 Apr 93 10:55:52 PDT X-To: comp.graphics.usenet X-Apparently-To: comp.graphics.usenet Anyone who thinks that TIFF is too complex hasn't dealt with CGM, ASN.1, CDA, DCA, SGML, or any one of a number of other very successful file format.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: People seem perfectly capable dealing with these others.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: Dealing with the format of TIFF is frankly less difficult than dealing with the DCT, LZW, and FAX encoding of the image data.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: The majority of the libraries which deal with TIFF are dedicated to these other issues rather than with simply decoding the tags and parameters.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: Perhaps people are overwhelmed in comparison with some rather simpleminded formats such as GIF, PCX, and BMP, but to suggest that TIFF is so complex as to be doomed to failure is ludicrous.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: That doesn't mean that GIF isn't fine, but don't even thing about using it in many instances.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: GIF is very nice for use in low-end photos applications and for screen grabs and such, but it would never do for high-volume or high-resolution systems.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: FAX is nice, but it doesn't do color (and GIF doesn't do B&W all that well).
label: comp.graphics

sentence: JPEG is nice for high-resolution color, but is slow for low-end.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: The advantage TIFF brings to the table is its ability to handle all these situations (and then some).
label: comp.graphics

sentence: Naturally it's more complex.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: But I'ld rather propose TIFF imaging solutions over imaging systems based on having to deal with 3-4 file formats anyday.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: You may find that TIFF is too complicated for your personal tastes but please don't wrail against it's complexity.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: The complexity it contains is required to provide the functionality it does and doesn't come close to the complexity found in most commercially viable file formats.
label: comp.graphics

sentence: If we're in philosophical arguments against complexity, let's all go program in scheme and forth and do imaging with run length encoding.
label: comp.graphics

label: sci.med

sentence: LOS ANGELES, April 28 -- A new method for diagnosing and measuring chemical imbalances in the brain which lead to Alzheimer's disease and other dementias has been discovered by researchers at the Huntington Medical Research Institutes (HMRI) in Pasadena, Calif.
label: sci.med

sentence: Results of their research will be reported in the May issue of the scientific journal, Radiology.
label: sci.med

sentence: Using an advanced form of magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) called magnetic resonance spectroscopy (MRS), a research team led by Brian D. Ross, M.D., D. Phil., conducted a study on 21 elderly patients who were believed to be suffering from some form of dementia.
label: sci.med

sentence: The exams used standard MRI equipment fitted with special software developed at HMRI called Clinical Proton MRS.
label: sci.med

sentence: Clinical Proton MRS is easily applied, giving doctors confirmatory diagnoses in less than 30 minutes.
label: sci.med

sentence: An automated version of Clinical Proton MRS called Proton Brain Examination (PROBE) reduces the examination time yet further, providing confirmatory diagnoses in less than 10 minutes.
label: sci.med

sentence: By comparison, the current "standard of care" in testing for Alzheimer's disease calls for lengthy memory function and neuropsychological tests, which can be very upsetting to the patient, are not definitive and can only be confirmed by autopsy.
label: sci.med

sentence: In addition to Alzheimer's disease, the new Clinical Proton MRS exam may have applications in diagnosing other dementias, including AIDS-related dementia, Parkinson's disease and Huntington's disease.
label: sci.med

sentence: "We've developed a simple test which can be administered quickly and relatively inexpensively using existing MRI equipment fitted with either the MRS or PROBE software," said Dr. Ross, adding, "this will help physicians to diagnose Alzheimer's earlier and intervene with therapeutics before the progression of the disease causes further damage to the delicate inner workings of the brain."
label: sci.med

sentence: Dr. Ross and his HMRI team measured a family of chemicals in the brain known as inositols, and myo-inositol (MI) acted as a marker in the study.
label: sci.med

sentence: In comparison to healthy patients, those diagnosed with Alzheimer's showed a 22 percent increase in MI, while their level of another chemical called N-acetylaspartate (NAA) was significantly lower, indicating a loss of brain-stimulating neurons believed to be associated with the progression of the disease.
label: sci.med

sentence: Current drug therapy for Alzheimer's disease is widely considered to be inadequate.
label: sci.med

sentence: This is attributable, Dr. Ross believes, to the theory that Alzheimer's is caused by an interruption in the transmission of the chemical acetylcholine to the nerve cells.
label: sci.med

sentence: This belief has been adhered to over the last 15 years, and consequently, most drugs to treat Alzheimer's were based on the changing receptors for acetylcholine.
label: sci.med

sentence: "Physicians have a real need for a test to differentiate Alzheimer's from other dementias, to provide the patient and his or her family with a firm diagnosis and to monitor future treatment protocols for the treatment of this disease.
label: sci.med

sentence: For this reason, we consider this test a major advancement in medicine," said Bruce Miller, M.D., a noted neurologist at Harbor-UCLA, MRS researcher and a co-author of the study.

sentence: HICNet Medical Newsletter Page 17 Volume 6, Number 11 April 25, 1993 :::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::: Articles :::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::: Research Shows Promise for Preventing or Slowing Blindness due to Retinal Disease National Retinitis Pigmentosa Foundation Neutrophilic Factors Rescue Photoreceptor Cells in Animal Tests Baltimore, MD - Researchers at the University of California San Francisco and Regeneron Pharmaceuticals, Inc.
label: sci.med

sentence: [NASDAQ: REGN] have discovered that certain naturally occurring substances known as neurotrophic factors can prevent the degeneration of light-sensing cells in the retina of the eye.
label: sci.med

sentence: The degeneration of these cells, known as photoreceptors, is a major cause of visual impairment This research, published to in the December issue of the Proceedings of the National Academy of Science (PNAS), holds promise for people who may lose their sight due to progressive retinal degeneration -- currently, no drug treatment for retinal degeneration exists.
label: sci.med

sentence: It is estimated that 2.5 million Americans have severe vision loss due to age-related macular degeneration and 100,000 Americans are affected by retinitis pigmentosus, a hereditary disease that causes blindness.
label: sci.med

sentence: In addition, each year more than 15,000 people undergo surgical procedures to repair retinal detachments and other retinal traumas.
label: sci.med

sentence: The research was funded in part by the RP (Retinitis Pigmentosa) Foundation Fighting Blindness, Regeneron Pharmaceuticals and the National Eye Institute.
label: sci.med

sentence: It was conducted by Drs.
label: sci.med

sentence: Matthew M. LaVail, Kazuhiko Unoki, Douglas Yasurnura, Michael T. Matthes and Roy H. Steinberg at UCSF, arld Dr. C;eorge Yancoooulos, Regeneron's Vice President for Discovery.
label: sci.med

sentence: Regeneron holds an exclusive license for this research from UCSF.
label: sci.med

sentence: In the research described in the PNAS , a light-damage model was used to assess the survival-promoting activity of a number of naturally occurring substances.
label: sci.med

sentence: Experimental rats were exposed to constant light for one week.
label: sci.med

sentence: Eyes that had not been treated with an effective factor lost most of their photoreceptor cells -- the rods and cones of the retina -- after light exposure.
label: sci.med

sentence: Brain Derived Neurotrophic Factor (BDNF) and Ciliary Neurotrophic Factor (CNTF) were particularly effective in this model without causing unwanted side effects; other factors such as Nerve Growth Factor (NGF) and Insulin-like Growth Factor (IGF-1) were not effective in these experiments.
label: sci.med

sentence: Discussing the research, Dr. Jesse M. Cedarbaum, Regeneron's Director of Clinical Research, said, "BDNF's ability to rescue neurons in the retina that have been damaged by light exposure may hold promise for the treatment of age- related macular degeneration, one of the leading causes of vision impairment, and for retinal detachment.
label: sci.med

sentence: Following detachment, permanent vision loss may HICNet Medical Newsletter Page 18 Volume 6, Number 11 April 25, 1993 result frorn the death of detached retinal cells.
label: sci.med

sentence: It is possible that BDNF could play a role in rescuing those cells once the retina has been reattached surgically."
label: sci.med

sentence: "Retinitis pigmentosa is a slowly progressing disease that causes the retina to degenerate over a period of years or even decades.
label: sci.med

sentence: Vision decreases to a small tunnel of sight and can result in total blindness.
label: sci.med

sentence: It is our hope that research on growth factors will provide a means to slow the progression and preserve useful vision throughout life," stated Jeanette S. Felix, Ph.D., Director of Science for the RP Foundation Fighting Blindness.

sentence: HICNet Medical Newsletter Page 19 Volume 6, Number 11 April 25, 1993 Affluent Diet Increases Risk Of Heart Disease Research Resources Reporter written by Mary Weideman Nov/Dec 1992 National Institutes of Health High-fat, high-calorie diets rapidly increase risk factors for coronary heart disease in native populations of developing countries that have traditionally consumed diets low in fat.
label: sci.med

sentence: These findings, according to investigators at the Oregon Health Sciences University in Portland, have serious implications for public health in both industrialized and developing countries.
label: sci.med

sentence: "This study demonstrates why we can develop coronary heart disease and have higher blood cholesterol and triglyceride levels.
label: sci.med

sentence: It shows also the importance of diet and particularly the potential of the diet to increase body weight, thereby leading to a whole host of other health problems in developing countries and affluent nations as well," explains principal investigator Dr. William E. Connor, head of the section of clinical nutrition and lipid metabolism at Oregon Health Sciences University.
label: sci.med

sentence: Over the past 25 years Dr. Connor and his team have characterized the food and nutrient intakes of the Tara humara Indians in Mexico, while simultaneously documenting various aspects of Tarahumara lipid metabolism.
label: sci.med

sentence: These native Mexicans number approximately 50,000 and reside in the Sierra Madre Occidental Mountains in the state of Chihuahua.
label: sci.med

sentence: The Tarahumaras have coupled an agrarian diet to endurance racing.
label: sci.med

sentence: Probably as a result, coronary heart disease, which is so prevalent in Western industrialized nations, is virtually non existent in their culture.
label: sci.med

sentence: Loosely translated, the name Tarahumara means "fleet of foot," reflecting a tribal passion for betting on "kickball" races, in which participants run distances of 100 miles or more while kicking a machete-carved wooden ball.
label: sci.med

sentence: The typical Tarahumara diet consists primarily of pinto beans, tortillas, and pinole, a drink made of ground roasted corn mixed with cold water, together with squash and gath ered fruits and vegetables.
label: sci.med

sentence: The Tara humaras also eat small amounts of game, fish, and eggs.
label: sci.med

sentence: Their food contains approximately 12 percent of total calories as fat of which the majority (69 percent) is of vegetable origin.
label: sci.med

sentence: Dietician Martha McMurry, a coinvestigator in the study, describes their diet as simple and very rich in nutrients while low in cholesterol and fat.
label: sci.med

sentence: The Tarahumaras have average plasma cholesterol levels of 121 mg/ dL, low-density lipoprotein (LDL)-cholesterol levels of 72 mg/dl, and high-density lipoprotein (HDL)-cholesterol levels of 32 to 42 mg/dl.
label: sci.med

sentence: All of those values are in the good, low-risk range, according to the researchers.
label: sci.med

sentence: Elevated cholesterol and LDL-cholesterol levels are considered risk factors for heart HICNet Medical Newsletter Page 20 Volume 6, Number 11 April 25, 1993 disease.
label: sci.med

sentence: HDL-cholesterol is considered beneficial.
label: sci.med

sentence: In previous studies the Tarahumaras had been found to be at low risk for cardiac disease, although able to respond to high-cholesterol diets with elevations in total and LDL- cholesterol.
label: sci.med

sentence: Clinical Research Center dietitian McMurry and coinvestigator Maria Teresa Cerqueira established a metabolic unit in a Jesuit mission school building near a community hospital in the small village of Sisoguichi.
label: sci.med

sentence: Food was weighed, cooked, and fed to the study participants under the investigators' direct supervision, ensuring that subjects ate only food stipulated by the research protocol.
label: sci.med

sentence: Fasting blood was drawn twice weekly, and plasma samples were frozen and shipped to Dr. Connors laboratory for cholesterol, triglyceride, and lipoprotein analyses.
label: sci.med

sentence: Regular measurements included participant body weight, height, and triceps skin fold thickness.
label: sci.med

sentence: Thirteen Tarahumaras, five women and eight men, including one adolescent, were fed their native diet for 1 week, followed by 5 weeks of an "affluent" diet.
label: sci.med

sentence: "In this study we went up to a concentration of dietary fat that was 40 percent of total calories.
label: sci.med

sentence: This is the prototype of the holiday diet that many Americans consume a diet high in fat, sugar, and cholesterol, low in fiber," elaborates Dr. Conners.
label: sci.med

sentence: Such dietary characteristics are reflected in the cholesterol-saturation index, or CSI, recently devised research dietitian Sonja Conner working with Dr. Connor.
label: sci.med

sentence: "The CSI is a single number that incorporates both the amount of cholesterol and the amount of saturated fat in the diet.
label: sci.med

sentence: CSI indicates the diet's potential to elevate the cholesterol level, particularly the LDL," Dr. Connor explains.
label: sci.med

sentence: The Tarahumaran diet averages a very low CSI of 20; Dr. Connor's "affluent" diet used in the study ranks a CSI of 149.
label: sci.med

sentence: The experimental design of this study reflects the importance of establishing baseline plasma lipid levels, typical of the native diet, before exposing subjects to the experimental diet.
label: sci.med

sentence: The standard curve relating dietary food intake to plasma cholesterol demonstrates a leveling off, or plateau, for consumption of large amounts of fat.
label: sci.med

sentence: Changes in dietary fat and/or cholesterol in this range have little effect on plasma levels.
label: sci.med

sentence: "You must have the baseline diet almost free of the variables you are going to put into the experimental diet.
label: sci.med

sentence: The Framingham study, for example, did not discriminate on the basis of diet between individuals who got heart disease because the diet was already high in fat.
label: sci.med

sentence: All subjects were already eating on a plateau," Dr. Connor says.
label: sci.med

sentence: After 5 weeks of consuming the "affluent" diet, the subjects' mean plasma cholesterol levels had in creased by 31 percent, primarily in the LDL fraction, which rose 39 percent.
label: sci.med

sentence: HDL-cholesterol increased by 31 per cent, and LDL to HDL ratios changed therefore very little.
label: sci.med

sentence: Plasma triglyceride levels increased by 18 percent, and subjects averaged an 8-pound gain in weight.
label: sci.med

sentence: According to Dr. Connor, lipid changes occurred surprisingly soon, yielding nearly the same results after 7 days of affluent diet as after 35 days.
label: sci.med

sentence: HICNet Medical Newsletter Page 21 Volume 6, Number 11 April 25, 1993
label: sci.med

sentence: The increase in HDL carries broad dietary implications for industrialized nations.
label: sci.med

sentence: "We think HDL-cholesterol increased because we increased the amount of dietary fat over the fat content used in the previous Tarahumara metabolic study.
label: sci.med

sentence: In that study we saw no change in HDL levels after raising the dietary cholesterol but keeping the fat relatively consistent with native consumption.
label: sci.med

sentence: In the present study we increased fat intake to 40 percent of the total calories.
label: sci.med

sentence: We reached the conclusion in the Tarahumara study that HDL reflects the amount of dietary fat in general and not the amount of dietary cholesterol.
label: sci.med

sentence: HDL must increase to help metabolize the fat, and it increased quite a bit in this study," Dr. Connor explains.
label: sci.med

sentence: Low HDL in the Tarahumarans is not typically an important predictor of coronary heart disease because they do not normally consume large amounts of fat or cholesterol.
label: sci.med

sentence: HDL remains an important predictor to Americans because of their usual high fat intake.
label: sci.med

sentence: Dr. Connor recommends a diet for Americans that contains less than 20 percent of total calories as fat, less than 100 mg of cholesterol, and a CSI around 20, varying in accordance with caloric needs.
label: sci.med

sentence: Such a diet is low in meat and dairy fat, high in fiber.
label: sci.med

sentence: Dr. Connor also comments on recent suggestions that Americans adopt a "Mediterranean-style" diet.
label: sci.med

sentence: "The original Mediterranean diet, in its pristine state, consisted of a very low intake of fat and very few animal and dairy products.
label: sci.med

sentence: We are already eating a lot of meat and dairy products.
label: sci.med

sentence: Simply to continue that pattern while switching to olive oil is not going to help the situation."
label: sci.med

sentence: The World Health Organization (WHO) is focusing much attention on the emergence of diseases such as coronary heart disease in nations and societies undergoing technological development.
label: sci.med

sentence: Dr. Connor says that coronary heart disease starts with a given society's elite, who typically eat a different diet than the average citizen.
label: sci.med

sentence: "If the pattern of afluence increases, the entire population will have have a higher incidence of coronary heart disease, which places a termendous health care burden on a society.
label: sci.med

sentence: WHO would like the developing countries to prevent coronary heart disease, so they can concentrate on other aspects of their economic development and on public health measures to improve general well-being, rather than paying for unnecessary, expensive medical technology," Dr. Connors says.
label: sci.med

sentence: "The overall implication of this study is that humans can readily move their plasma lipids and lipoprotein values into a high-risk range within a very short time by an affluent, excessive diet.
label: sci.med

sentence: The present rate of coronary heart disease in the United States is 30 percent less than it was 20 years ago, so a lot has been accomplished.
label: sci.med

sentence: We are changing rapidly," he concludes.

Indurkhya TUTORIAL PROGRAM Tutorials will be conducted at the Bethesda Ramada Hotel on Tuesday, July 6. 12:00-2:45pm "Introduction to Molecular Biology for Computer Scientists" Prof. Mick Noordewier (Rutgers University)
label: sci.med

sentence: This overview of the essential facts of molecular biology is intended as an introduction to the field for computer scientists who wish to apply their tools to this rich and complex domain.
label: sci.med

sentence: Material covered will include structural and informational molecules, the basic organization of the cell and of genetic material, the "central dogma" of gene expression, and selected other topics in the area of structure, function, and regulation as relates to current computational approaches.
label: sci.med

sentence: Dr. Noordewier has appointments in both Computer Science and Biology at Rutgers, and has extensive experience in basic biological research in addition to his current work in computational biology.
label: sci.med

sentence: 12:00-2:45pm "Introduction to Artificial Intelligence for Biologists" Dr. Richard Lathrop (MIT & Arris Corp.)
label: sci.med

sentence: An overview of the field of artificial intelligence will be presented, as it relates to actual and potential biological applications.
label: sci.med

sentence: Fundamental techniques, symbolic programming languages, and notions of search will be discussed, as well as selected topics in somewhat greater detail, such as knowledge representation, inference, and machine learning.
label: sci.med

sentence: The intended audience includes biologists with some computational background, but no extensive exposure to artificial intelligence.
label: sci.med

sentence: Dr. Lathrop, co-developer of ARIADNE and related technologies, has worked in the area of artificial intelligence applied to biological problems in both academia and industry.
label: sci.med

sentence: 3:00-5:45pm "Neural Networks, Statistics, and Information Theory in Biological Sequence Analysis" Dr. Alan Lapedes (Los Alamos National Laboratory)
label: sci.med

sentence: This tutorial will cover the most rapidly-expanding facet of intelligent systems for molecular biology, that of machine learning techniques applied to sequence analysis.
label: sci.med

sentence: Closely interrelated topics to be addressed include the use of artifical neural networks to elicit both specific signals and general characteristics of sequences, and the relationship of such approaches to statistical techniques and information-theoretic views of sequence data.
label: sci.med

sentence: Dr. Lapedes, of the Theoretical Division at Los Alamos, has long been a leader in the use of such techniques in this domain.
label: sci.med

sentence: 3:00-5:45pm "Genetic Algorithms and Genetic Programming" Prof. John Koza (Stanford University)
label: sci.med

sentence: The genetic algorithm, an increasingly popular approach to highly non-linear multi-dimensional optimization problems, was originally inspired by a biological metaphor.
label: sci.med

sentence: This tutorial will cover both the biological motivations, and the actual implementation and characteristics of the algorithm.
label: sci.med

sentence: Genetic Programming, an extension well-suited to problems where the discovery of the size and shape of the solution is a major part of the problem, will also be addressed.
label: sci.med

sentence: Particular attention will be paid to biological applications, and to identifying resources and software that will permit attendees to begin using the methods.
label: sci.med

sentence: Dr. Koza, a Consulting Professor of Computer Science at Stanford, has taught this subject since 1988 and is the author of a standard text in the field.
label: sci.med

sentence: 3:00-5:45pm "Linguistic Methods in Sequence Analysis" Prof. David Searls (University of Pennsylvania) & Shmuel Pietrokovski (Weizmann Institute) Approaches to sequence analysis based on linguistic methodologies are increasingly in evidence.
label: sci.med

sentence: These involve the adaptation of tools and techniques from computational linguistics for syntactic pattern recognition and gene prediction, the classification of genetic structures and phenomena using formal language theory, the identification of significant vocabularies and overlapping codes in sequence data, and sequence comparison reflecting taxonomic and functional relatedness.
label: sci.med

sentence: Dr. Searls, who holds research faculty appointments in both Genetics and Computer Science at Penn, represents the branch of this field that considers higher-order syntactic approaches to sequence data, while Shmuel Pietrokovski has studied and published with Prof. Edward Trifinov in the area of word-based analyses.
label: sci.med

sentence: The "HyperKnowledge" PROJECT for NeXTSTEP Motivation We are a heterogeneous group of scientists and students who feel that our work is continuously hindered by computer environments dominated by incompatible scientific tools and monstrous software packages (too often claiming to do everything).
label: sci.med

sentence: Rather than being able to use different tools together in a flexible, interactive system, we find ourselves spending too much time converting between different data formats, writing throw-away tools and I/O parsers, and worrying about how to get to a particular goal rather than what it means to have attained it.
label: sci.med

sentence: What we need is an object-oriented scientific environment where the tools we choose to use are integrated without being parts of a closed system, highly interactive, and extendable (both by the addition of our own specialized objects and by combining the available tools - graphically).
label: sci.med

sentence: The use of such an environment should be a natural extension of our work, requiring a very short learning phase and practically no user-documentation.
label: sci.med

sentence: The user should feel encouraged to explore different possibilities, testing his/her own scientific ideas without worrying too much about whether the system is able to cope (within reason, of course).
label: sci.med

sentence: By building an open and object-oriented system, each user should be able to draw upon and combine those tools necessary or conducive to a particular task: we all need to organize and archive our data, display results, and combine information from many different sources.
label: sci.med

sentence: Currently, the group consists of people from very different fields: Molecular Biology, Computer Science, Physics and Astronomy, and Geography.
label: sci.med

sentence: While many of our needs for such an environment diverge, the underlying motivation is the same: no matter what you want to call it, we need a system which helps rather than hinders the organization of our scientific data and daily work.
label: sci.med

sentence: Why NeXTSTEP?
label: sci.med

sentence: What else is there?
label: sci.med

sentence: No other system offers the same power, a totally object-oriented developers environment, UNIX-compatibility, highly-interactive and standardized user-interface.
label: sci.med

sentence: With the advent of NS486 and the expectation that NeXTSTEP will soon appear on a broad range of hardware platforms, such a system will soon be nearly universally available.
label: sci.med

sentence: Classical workstation vendors (SUN, IBM, HP, and the rest) now have little to offer, no similar tools exist for the standard X-Windows GUI, if Apple had a system it would only run on their hardware, and the mass-market Windows/DOS world is a developer's nightmare (either despite, or more probably because of, OLE).

sentence: The UL stands for "Underwriters Laboratory".

sentence: It used to be an Insurance Industry organization, but now it is independent and non-profit.
label: sci.electronics

sentence: It tests electrical components and equipment for potential hazards.

sentence: When something is UL-listed, that means that the UL has tested the device, and it meets their requirements for safety - ie: fire or shock hazard.
label: sci.electronics

sentence: It doesn't necessarily mean that the device actually does what it's supposed to, just that it probably won't kill you.

sentence: The UL does not have power of law in the U.S. -- you are permitted to buy and install non-UL-listed devices.

sentence: However, insurance policies sometimes have clauses in them that will limit their liability in case of a claim made in response to the failure of a non-UL-listed device.

sentence: Furthermore, in many situations the NEC will require that a wiring component used for a specific purpose is UL-listed for that purpose.

sentence: Most jurisdictions require that you obtain a permit and inspections of any wiring that is done.

sentence: Fuses and circuit breakers are designed to interrupt the power to a circuit when the current flow exceeds safe levels.

sentence: For example, if your toaster shorts out, a fuse or breaker should "trip", protecting the wiring in the walls from melting.

sentence: Breakers are fairly complicated mechanical devices.

sentence: They usually consist of one spring loaded contact which is latched into position against another contact.

sentence: When the current flow through the device exceeds the rated value, a bimetallic strip heats up and bends.

sentence: By bending it "trips" the latch, and the spring pulls the contacts apart.

sentence: Circuit breakers behave similarly to fusetrons - that is, they tend to take longer to trip at moderate overloads than ordinary fuses.

sentence: With high overloads, they trip quickly.

sentence: A GFCI is a ``ground-fault circuit interrupter''.

sentence: It measures the current current flowing through the hot wire and the neutral wire.
label: sci.electronics

sentence: If they differ by more than a few milliamps, the presumption is that current is leaking to ground via some other path.

sentence: - Ungrounded grounding outlets (except when downstream of a GFCI) - Splices hanging in mid-air (other than proper knob-and-tube) - Switched neutrals - Unsecured Romex swinging about like grapevines Certain wiring practises that are actually to code (or were at one time) sometimes reveal DIY wiring that may have hidden violations: - Switches that seem to control nothing (abandoned, perhaps not properly terminated wiring) -
label: sci.electronics

sentence: A wall switch that shuts off a group of lights that are separately controlled by other wall switches.
label: sci.electronics

sentence: (except when it's *really* convenient ;-) - Switches and outlets in bizarre locations - Great numbers of junction boxes without outlets or lamps - Junction boxes with great numbers of wires going into them - Wiring that passes through a closet instead of a wall or ceiling - Backwrapped grounding wires In the years since Edison "invented" electricity, several different wiring "styles" have come and gone.
label: sci.electronics

sentence: When you buy an older home you may encounter some of this stuff.

sentence: This section describes the old methods, and some of their idiosyncrasies.
label: sci.electronics

sentence: The oldest wiring system you're likely to encounter is called "knob and tube" (K&T).
label: sci.electronics

sentence: It is made up of individual conductors with a cloth insulation.
label: sci.electronics

sentence: The wires are run along side structural members (eg: joists or studs) using ceramic stand-offs (knobs).
label: sci.electronics

sentence: Wire is run through structural members using ceramic tubes.
label: sci.electronics

sentence: Connections were made by twisting the wire together, soldering, and wrapping with tape.
label: sci.electronics

sentence: Since the hot and neutral were run separately, the wiring tends to be rather confusing.
label: sci.electronics

sentence: A neutral often runs down the centre of each room, with "taps" off to each fixture.
label: sci.electronics

sentence: The hot wire tended to run from one fixture to the next.
label: sci.electronics

sentence: In some cases K&T isn't colour-coded, so the neutral is often the same colour as the hot wires.
label: sci.electronics

sentence: You'll see K&T in homes built as late as the 40's.
label: sci.electronics

sentence: Comments on K&T: - the people installing K&T were pretty paranoid about electricity, so the workmanship tends to be pretty good.
label: sci.electronics

sentence: - The wire, insulation and insulators tend to stand up very well.
label: sci.electronics

sentence: Most K&T I've seen, for example, is in quite good condition.
label: sci.electronics

sentence: No grounding.
label: sci.electronics

sentence: Grounding is usually difficult to install.
label: sci.electronics

sentence: - boxes are small.
label: sci.electronics

sentence: Receptacle replacement (particularly with GFCI) can be difficult.
label: sci.electronics

sentence: No bushing on boxes either, so wiring changes need special attention to box entry.
label: sci.electronics

sentence: - Sometimes the neutral isn't balanced very well between separately hot circuits, so it is sometimes possible to overload the neutral without exceeding the fusing on any circuit.
label: sci.electronics

sentence: - Building code does not permit insulation in walls that contain K&T. - Connection to existing K&T from new circuits can be tricky.
label: sci.electronics

sentence: Consult your inspector.
label: sci.electronics

sentence: - Modern wiring practise requires considerably more outlets to be installed than K&T systems did.
label: sci.electronics

sentence: Since K&T tends to be in pretty decent condition it generally isn't necessary to replace it simply because it's K&T.
label: sci.electronics

sentence: What you should watch out for is renovations that have interfered with it and be cautious about circuit loading.

sentence: In many cases it's perfectly reasonable to leave existing K&T alone, and add new fixtures on new circuits using modern techniques.

sentence: EDU.AU (-s87271077-s.walker-man-50-) wrote: Except for their size, the cooling towers on nuclear power plants are vertually identical in construction and operation to cooling towers designed and built in the 1890's (a hundred years ago) for coal fired power plants used for lighting and early electric railways.
label: sci.electronics

sentence: Basicly, the cylindrical tower supports a rapid air draft when its air is heated by hot water and/or steam circulating thru a network of pipes that fill about the lower 1/3 of the tower.
label: sci.electronics

sentence: To assist cooling and the draft, water misters are added that spray cold water over the hot pipes.
label: sci.electronics

sentence: The cold water evaporates, removing the heat faster than just air flow from the draft would and the resulting water vapor is rapidly carried away by the draft.
label: sci.electronics

sentence: This produces the clouds frequently seen rising out of these towers.
label: sci.electronics

sentence: That slight pinch (maybe 2/3 of the way up the tower) is there because it produces a very significant increase in the strength and rate of the air draft produced, compared to a straight cylinder shape.
label: sci.electronics

sentence: The towers are used to recondense the steam in the sealed steam system of the power plant so that it can be recirculated back to the boiler and used again.
label: sci.electronics

sentence: The wider the temperature difference across the turbines used in the power plant the more effecient they are and by recondensing the steam in the cooling towers before sending it back to the boilers you maintain a very wide temperature difference (sometimes as high as 1000 degrees or more from first stage "hot" turbine to final stage "cold" turbine).

sentence: The problem is that Motif uses XGrabKey to implement menu accelerators, and these grabs are specific about which modifiers apply.

sentence: Unfortunately, the specification for XGrabKey doesn't allow AnyModifier to be combined with other modifiers, which is exactly what would be desired in this case: "Ctrl Any<Key>q".
label: comp.windows.x

sentence: This is true for accelerators and mnemonics, which are implemented using event handlers instead of grabs; it's not true for menu accelerators.

sentence: In addition to the normal menu accelerator you install on the XmPushButton[Gadget], set an XtNaccelerators resource on the shell (TopLevel or Application).

sentence: Install the shell's accelerators on itself and all of its descendants with XtInstallAllAccelerators (shell, shell).

label: comp.windows.x

label: comp.windows.x

sentence: Exactly *why* the two components get into this sulk is unclear to |> me; all information greatly received.
label: comp.windows.x

sentence: This problem is caused by a bad interaction between the way olwm reparents windows, a misfeature of the X protocol, and a bug in the Xt intrinsics.
label: comp.windows.x

sentence: The Intrinsics bug is that it ignores the (x,y) position given in ReparentNotify events.
label: comp.windows.x

sentence: Olwm is structured in such a way that, when a window is popped down, the last event the client receives is a ReparentNotify event.
label: comp.windows.x

sentence: At this point, the Intrinsics' notion of the shell's location is out of date.
label: comp.windows.x

sentence: If a SetValues is done using the same (x,y) position, the Intrinsics thinks it needs to move the shell, so it issues a ConfigureWindow request to move it there.
label: comp.windows.x

sentence: However, a (mis)feature of the X protocol is that a ConfigureWindow request that has no effect will generate no ConfigureNotify event.
label: comp.windows.x

sentence: The Intrinsics thus waits for an event that will never arrive, and times out after wmTimeout has expired.
label: comp.windows.x

sentence: (Actually, things are somewhat more complex because the request is redirected to the window manager, but the net result is the same.)
label: comp.windows.x

sentence: The solution is for the Intrinsics to note the (x,y) positioning information in ReparentNotify events.
label: comp.windows.x

sentence: This has been implemented and is available in X11R5 public fix 20.
label: comp.windows.x

sentence: This is XBUG 5513 for those of you who are interested in gorier details.

label: comp.windows.x

sentence: X X You are encouraged to examine the winners of previous contests.

sentence: X X
label: comp.windows.x

sentence: An entry is usually examined in a number of ways.
label: comp.windows.x

sentence: We typically apply X a number of tests to an entry: X X * look at the original source X * convert ANSI tri-graphs to ASCII X * C pre-process the source ignoring '#include' lines
label: comp.windows.x

sentence: X * C pre-process the source ignoring '#define' and '#include' lines X * run it through a C beautifier X * examine the algorithm X

sentence: * lint it X * compile it X
label: comp.windows.x

sentence: * execute it X X
label: comp.windows.x

sentence: You should consider how your entry looks in each of the above tests.
label: comp.windows.x

sentence: X
label: comp.windows.x

sentence: You should ask yourself if your entry remains obscure after it has been X 'cleaned up' by the C pre-processor and a C beautifier.
label: comp.windows.x

sentence: X X
label: comp.windows.x

sentence: Your entry need not do well under all, or in most tests.
label: comp.windows.x

sentence: In certain X cases, a test is not important.
label: comp.windows.x

sentence: Entries that compete for the X 'strangest/most creative source layout' need not do as well as X others in terms of their algorithm.
label: comp.windows.x

sentence: On the other hand, given X two such entries, we are more inclined to pick the entry that X does something interesting when you run it.
label: comp.windows.x

sentence: X X
label: comp.windows.x

sentence: We try to avoid limiting creativity in our rules.
label: comp.windows.x

sentence: As such, we leave X the contest open for creative rule interpretation.
label: comp.windows.x

sentence: As in real life X programming, interpreting a requirements document or a customer request X is important.
label: comp.windows.x

sentence: For this reason, we often award 'worst abuse of the X rules' to an entry that illustrates this point in an ironic way.
label: comp.windows.x

sentence: X X
label: comp.windows.x

sentence: If you do plan to abuse the rules, we suggest that you let us know X in the remarks section.
label: comp.windows.x

sentence: Please note that an invitation to abuse X is not an invitation to break.
label: comp.windows.x

sentence: We are strict when it comes to the X 3217 byte size limit.
label: comp.windows.x

sentence: Also, abusing the entry format tends to X annoy more than amuse.
label: comp.windows.x

sentence: X X
label: comp.windows.x

sentence: We do realize that there are holes in the rules, and invite entries X to attempt to exploit them.
label: comp.windows.x

sentence: We will award 'worst abuse of the rules' X and then plug the hole next year.
label: comp.windows.x

sentence: Even so, we will attempt to use X the smallest plug needed, if not smaller. :-)

sentence: X X Check out your program and be sure that it works.

sentence: We sometimes make X the effort to debug an entry that has a slight problem, particularly X in or near the final round.

sentence: On the other hand, we have seen some X of the best entries fall down because they didn't work.

sentence: X X
label: comp.windows.x

sentence: We tend to look down on a prime number printer, that claims that X 16 is a prime number.
label: comp.windows.x

sentence: If you do have a bug, you are better off X documenting it.
label: comp.windows.x

sentence: Noting "this entry sometimes prints the 4th power X of a prime by mistake" would save the above entry.
label: comp.windows.x

sentence: And sometimes, X a strange bug/feature can even help the entry!
label: comp.windows.x

sentence: Of course, a correctly X working entry is best.

sentence: X X XOUR LIKES AND DISLIKES: X X Doing masses of #defines to obscure the source has become 'old'.
label: comp.windows.x

sentence: We X tend to 'see thru' masses of #defines due to our pre-processor tests X that we apply.
label: comp.windows.x

sentence: Simply abusing #defines or -Dfoo=bar won't go as far X as a program that is more well rounded in confusion.
label: comp.windows.x

sentence: X X
label: comp.windows.x

sentence: Many ANSI C compilers dislike the following code, and so do we: X X #define d define X #d foo <-- don't expect this to turn into #define foo X X int i; X j; <-- don't use such implicit type declaration X int k; X X
label: comp.windows.x

sentence: We suggest that you compile your entry with an ANSI C compiler.
label: comp.windows.x

sentence: If you X must use non-ANSI C, such as K&R C, you must avoid areas that result in X compile/link errors for ANSI C compilers.
label: comp.windows.x

sentence: X X
label: comp.windows.x

sentence: Unfortunately, ANSI C requires array indexes to be of integral type.
label: comp.windows.x

sentence: X
label: comp.windows.x

sentence: Thus, the following classical obfuscation hacks are no longer allowed: X X int i; X char *c; X i[c]; <--- use c[i] instead X (i+3)["string"]; <--- use "string"[i+3] instead X X
label: comp.windows.x

sentence: If your entry uses functions that have a variable number of X arguments, be careful.
label: comp.windows.x

sentence: Systems implement va_list as a wide variety X of ways.
label: comp.windows.x

sentence: Because of this, a number of operations using va_list are X not portable and must not be used: X X * assigning a non-va_list variable to/from a va_list variable X * casting a non-va_list variable into/from a va_list variable X * passing a va_list variable to a function expecting a non-va_list arg X * passing a non-va_list variable to a function expecting a va_list arg X * performing arithmetic on va_list variables X
label: comp.windows.x

sentence: * using va_list as a structure or union X X In particular, do not treat va_list variables as if they were a char **'s.
label: comp.windows.x

sentence: X X Avoid using <varargs.h>, use <stdarg.h> instead.
label: comp.windows.x

sentence: X X
label: comp.windows.x

sentence: If you use C preprocessor directives (#define, #if, #ifdef, ...), X the leading '#' must be the first character on a line.
label: comp.windows.x

sentence: While some X C preprocessors allow whitespace the leading '#', many do not.
label: comp.windows.x

sentence: X X
label: comp.windows.x

sentence: Because the exit() function returns void on some systems, entries X must not assume that it returns an int.

sentence: X X Small programs are best when they are short, obscure and concise.
label: comp.windows.x

sentence: X
label: comp.windows.x

sentence: While such programs are not as complex as other winners, they do X serve a useful purpose.
label: comp.windows.x

sentence: They are often the only program that people X attempt to completely understand.
label: comp.windows.x

sentence: For this reason, we look for X programs that are compact, and are instructional.

sentence: X X One line programs should be short one line programs, say around 80 X bytes long.

sentence: Getting close to 160 bytes is a bit too long in our opinion.

sentence: X X

sentence: We tend to dislike programs that: X X * are very hardware specific X * are very OS or Un*x version specific X (index/strchr differences are ok, but socket/streams specific X code is likely not to be) X * dump core or have compiler warnings X (it is ok only if you warn us in the 'remark' header item)

sentence: The story begins: When Julius Caesar sent messages to his trusted acquaintances, he didn't trust the messengers.
label: sci.crypt

sentence: So he replaced every A by a C, every B by a D, and so on through the alphabet.
label: sci.crypt

sentence: Only someone who knew the ``shift by 2'' rule could decipher his messages.
label: sci.crypt

sentence: A cryptosystem or cipher system is a method of disguising messages so that only certain people can see through the disguise.
label: sci.crypt

sentence: Cryptography is the art of creating and using cryptosystems.
label: sci.crypt

sentence: Cryptanalysis is the art of breaking cryptosystems---seeing through the disguise even when you're not supposed to be able to.
label: sci.crypt

sentence: Cryptology is the study of both cryptography and cryptanalysis.
label: sci.crypt

sentence: The original message is called a plaintext.
label: sci.crypt

sentence: The disguised message is called a ciphertext.
label: sci.crypt

sentence: Encryption means any procedure to convert plaintext into ciphertext.
label: sci.crypt

sentence: Decryption means any procedure to convert ciphertext into plaintext.
label: sci.crypt

sentence: A cryptosystem is usually a whole collection of algorithms.
label: sci.crypt

sentence: The algorithms are labelled; the labels are called keys.
label: sci.crypt

sentence: For instance, Caesar probably used ``shift by n'' encryption for several different values of n.
label: sci.crypt

sentence: It's natural to say that n is the key here.
label: sci.crypt

sentence: The people who are supposed to be able to see through the disguise are called recipients.
label: sci.crypt

sentence: Other people are enemies, opponents, interlopers, eavesdroppers, or third parties.
label: sci.crypt

sentence: What references can I start with to learn cryptology?
label: sci.crypt

sentence: For an introduction to technical matter, the survey articles given in part 10 are the best place to begin as they are, in general, concise, authored by competent people, and well written.
label: sci.crypt

sentence: However, these articles are mostly concerned with cryptology as it has developed in the last 50 years or so, and are more abstract and mathematical than historical.
label: sci.crypt

sentence: The Codebreakers by Kahn [KAH67] is encyclopedic in its history and technical detail of cryptology up to the mid-60's.

sentence: [GAI44] or Sinkov [SIN66].

sentence: This is recommended especially for people who want to devise their own encryption algorithms since it is a common mistake to try to make a system before knowing how to break one.
label: sci.crypt

label: sci.crypt

sentence: Consequently several textbooks and books to serve as texts have appeared.
label: sci.crypt

sentence: The book of Denning [DEN82] gives a good introduction to a broad range of security including encryption algorithms, database security, access control, and formal models of security.
label: sci.crypt

sentence: Similar comments apply to the books of Price & Davies
label: sci.crypt

sentence: [PRI84] and Pfleeger [PFL89].
label: sci.crypt

sentence: The books of Konheim [KON81] and Meyer & Matyas [MEY82] are quite technical books.
label: sci.crypt

sentence: Both Konheim and Meyer were directly involved in the development of DES, and both books give a thorough analysis of DES.
label: sci.crypt

sentence: Konheim's book is quite mathematical, with detailed analyses of many classical cryptosystems.
label: sci.crypt

sentence: Meyer and Matyas concentrate on modern cryptographic methods, especially pertaining to key management and the integration of security facilities into computer systems and networks.
label: sci.crypt

sentence: The books of Rueppel [RUE86] and Koblitz [KOB89] concentrate on the application of number theory and algebra to cryptography.
label: sci.crypt

sentence: How does one go about cryptanalysis?
label: sci.crypt

sentence: Classical cryptanalysis involves an interesting combination of analytical reasoning, application of mathematical tools, pattern finding, patience, determination, and luck.
label: sci.crypt

sentence: The best available textbooks on the subject are the Military Cryptanalytics series [FRIE1].
label: sci.crypt

sentence: It is clear that proficiency in cryptanalysis is, for the most part, gained through the attempted solution of given systems.
label: sci.crypt

sentence: Such experience is considered so valuable that some of the cryptanalyses performed during WWII by the Allies are still classified.
label: sci.crypt

sentence: Modern public-key cryptanalysis may consist of factoring an integer, or taking a discrete logarithm.
label: sci.crypt

sentence: These are not the traditional fare of the cryptanalyst.
label: sci.crypt

sentence: Computational number theorists are some of the most successful cryptanalysts against public key systems.
label: sci.crypt

sentence: What is a brute-force search and what is its cryptographic relevance?
label: sci.crypt

sentence: In a nutshell: If f(x) = y
label: sci.crypt

sentence: and you know y and can compute f, you can find x by trying every possible x.
label: sci.crypt

sentence: That's brute-force search.
label: sci.crypt

sentence: Example: Say a cryptanalyst has found a plaintext and a corresponding ciphertext, but doesn't know the key.
label: sci.crypt

sentence: He can simply try encrypting the plaintext using each possible key, until the ciphertext matches---or decrypting the ciphertext to match the plaintext, whichever is faster.
label: sci.crypt

sentence: Every well-designed cryptosystem has such a large key space that this brute-force search is impractical.
label: sci.crypt

sentence: Advances in technology sometimes change what is considered practical.
label: sci.crypt

sentence: For example, DES, which has been in use for over 10 years now, has 2^56, or about 10^17, possible keys.
label: sci.crypt

sentence: A computation with this many operations was certainly unlikely for most users in the mid-70's.
label: sci.crypt

sentence: The situation is very different today given the dramatic decrease in cost per processor operation.
label: sci.crypt

sentence: Massively parallel machines threaten the security of DES against brute force search.
label: sci.crypt

sentence: Some scenarios are described by Garron and Outerbridge
label: sci.crypt

sentence: One phase of a more sophisticated cryptanalysis may involve a brute-force search of some manageably small space of possibilities.
label: sci.crypt

sentence: What are some properties satisfied by every strong cryptosystem?
label: sci.crypt

sentence: The security of a strong system resides with the secrecy of the key rather than with an attempt to keep the algorithm itself secret.
label: sci.crypt

sentence: A strong cryptosystem has a large keyspace, as mentioned above.
label: sci.crypt

sentence: The unicity distance is a measure which gives the minimum amount of ciphertext that must be intercepted to uniquely identify the key and if for some key, the unicity distance is much longer than the amount of ciphertext you intend to encrypt under that key, the system is probably strong.
label: sci.crypt

sentence: A strong cryptosystem will certainly produce ciphertext which appears random to all standard statistical tests (see, for example, [CAE90]).
label: sci.crypt

sentence: A strong cryptosystem will resist all known previous attacks.
label: sci.crypt

sentence: A system which has never been subjected to scrutiny is suspect.
label: sci.crypt

sentence: If a system passes all the tests mentioned above, is it necessarily strong?
label: sci.crypt

sentence: Certainly not.
label: sci.crypt

sentence: Many weak cryptosystems looked good at first.
label: sci.crypt

sentence: However, sometimes it is possible to show that a cryptosystem is strong by mathematical proof.
label: sci.crypt

sentence: ``If Joe can break this system, then he can also solve the well-known difficult problem of factoring integers.''
label: sci.crypt

sentence: See part 6.
label: sci.crypt

sentence: Failing that, it's a crap shoot.

sentence: If a cryptosystem is theoretically unbreakable, then is it guaranteed analysis-proof in practice?
label: sci.crypt

sentence: Cryptanalytic methods include what is known as ``practical cryptanalysis'': the enemy doesn't have to just stare at your ciphertext until he figures out the plaintext.
label: sci.crypt

sentence: For instance, he might assume ``cribs''---stretches of probable plaintext.
label: sci.crypt

sentence: If the crib is correct then he might be able to deduce the key and then decipher the rest of the message.
label: sci.crypt

sentence: Or he might exploit ``isologs''---the same plaintext enciphered in several cryptosystems or several keys.
label: sci.crypt

sentence: Thus he might obtain solutions even when cryptanalytic theory says he doesn't have a chance.
label: sci.crypt

sentence: Sometimes, cryptosystems malfunction or are misused.
label: sci.crypt

sentence: The one-time pad, for example, loses all security if it is used more than once!
label: sci.crypt

sentence: Even chosen-plaintext attacks, where the enemy somehow feeds plaintext into the encryptor until he can deduce the key, have been employed.
label: sci.crypt

label: rec.autos

sentence: I'd like to give some feedback based on real life experince.
label: rec.autos

sentence: I keep the Valentine-1 in advanced logic mode and it rarely lights up as a Christmas tree.
label: rec.autos

sentence: The only time it does is when I am in the middle of a major shopping area and then it makes sense that is does since there are >= 8 sources coming from many different directions.
label: rec.autos

sentence: I have found the Valentine-1 to be consistent in its reporting of bogeys regardless of any moving cars in the area.
label: rec.autos

sentence: I have found the directional indication to be very useful.
label: rec.autos

sentence: In one case there was two radar traps set up within one mile of each other.
label: rec.autos

sentence: As I passed the first radar trap, the direction indication changed.
label: rec.autos

sentence: Then the detector was set off again pointing in the forward direction.
label: rec.autos

sentence: With other radar detectors I would have assumed that this was due to a reflection.
label: rec.autos

sentence: But with the Valentine-1 I knew there was a high probability that there was another trap.
label: rec.autos

sentence: And there was!
label: rec.autos

sentence: On other occasions, the directional helped discern a false alarm from a true alarm.
label: rec.autos

sentence: For example, as I pass a source, the direction indicator changes.
label: rec.autos

sentence: The directional also allows me to focus my attention as to where the signal might be coming from instead of having to look all over the place.
label: rec.autos

sentence: When a car is approaching me from the rear with a detector that leaks, I can tell that the signal is coming from the rear and as the car passes me I can verify the source.
label: rec.autos

sentence: With other detectors, I would have been unable to do this and would have had to assume that there was a radar trap when there was none.
label: rec.autos

sentence: I've had the Valentine-1 for several months now and find its added features to be useful and not gimmicks.

sentence: The "HyperKnowledge" PROJECT for NeXTSTEP Motivation We are a heterogeneous group of scientists and students who feel that our work is continuously hindered by computer environments dominated by incompatible scientific tools and monstrous software packages (too often claiming to do everything).
label: sci.space

sentence: Rather than being able to use different tools together in a flexible, interactive system, we find ourselves spending too much time converting between different data formats, writing throw-away tools and I/O parsers, and worrying about how to get to a particular goal rather than what it means to have attained it.
label: sci.space

sentence: What we need is an object-oriented scientific environment where the tools we choose to use are integrated without being parts of a closed system, highly interactive, and extendable (both by the addition of our own specialized objects and by combining the available tools - graphically).
label: sci.space

sentence: The use of such an environment should be a natural extension of our work, requiring a very short learning phase and practically no user-documentation.
label: sci.space

sentence: The user should feel encouraged to explore different possibilities, testing his/her own scientific ideas without worrying too much about whether the system is able to cope (within reason, of course).
label: sci.space

sentence: By building an open and object-oriented system, each user should be able to draw upon and combine those tools necessary or conducive to a particular task: we all need to organize and archive our data, display results, and combine information from many different sources.
label: sci.space

sentence: Currently, the group consists of people from very different fields: Molecular Biology, Computer Science, Physics and Astronomy, and Geography.
label: sci.space

sentence: While many of our needs for such an environment diverge, the underlying motivation is the same: no matter what you want to call it, we need a system which helps rather than hinders the organization of our scientific data and daily work.
label: sci.space

sentence: Why NeXTSTEP?
label: sci.space

sentence: What else is there?
label: sci.space

sentence: No other system offers the same power, a totally object-oriented developers environment, UNIX-compatibility, highly-interactive and standardized user-interface.
label: sci.space

sentence: With the advent of NS486 and the expectation that NeXTSTEP will soon appear on a broad range of hardware platforms, such a system will soon be nearly universally available.
label: sci.space

sentence: Classical workstation vendors (SUN, IBM, HP, and the rest) now have little to offer, no similar tools exist for the standard X-Windows GUI, if Apple had a system it would only run on their hardware, and the mass-market Windows/DOS world is a developer's nightmare (either despite, or more probably because of, OLE).
label: sci.space

sentence: In order to progress, we need an open discussion of how best to develop such an environment.

sentence: In NeXTSTEP parlance, do we simply have to put together a set of scientific protocols and a common API for our "Hyper-knowledge" servers?

sentence: Should be use distributed objects?

sentence: What minimum set of tools do we need in order to start working with the environment?

sentence: What tool-needs do we all have in common and which ones are best developed by specialized sub-groups?

sentence: How many already existing tools can/should be integrated into the system (e.g. Mathematica)?
