sentence: Redesign Activities Update -- Following is the weekly status on redesign, based on information provided by NASA headquarters.
label: sci.space

sentence: The station Redesign Team (SRT) provided a detailed status report to the Advisory Committee on the Redesign of the Space Station on April 22.
label: sci.space

sentence: The day-long meeting was held in ANSER facilities in Crystal City, VA; topics covered by the SRT included a preliminary mission and goals statement for the space station; science, technology and engineering research; the assessment process; and the design approach.
label: sci.space

sentence: Discussions on management options and operations concepts also were held.
label: sci.space

sentence: The Design Teams then presented the three options under study: Option A - Modular Buildup -- Pete Priest presented the A option.
label: sci.space

sentence: Priest said the team is working to define a station that meets cost goals and has identified three distinct phases of evolution - power station, human tended and permanent presence.
label: sci.space

sentence: The team will define the minimum capability needed to achieve each phase, the total cost of each phase and the achievable capability for budget levels.
label: sci.space

sentence: The A option uses current or simplified Freedom hardware where cost effective and is considering other existing systems such as the so-called "Bus-1 spacecraft," the orbiter and Spacelab.
label: sci.space

sentence: The Power Station Capability could be achieved in 3 flights with Freedom photo voltaic modules providing 20 kW of power.
label: sci.space

sentence: 30-day Shuttle/Spacelab missions docked to the power station are assumed for this phase.
label: sci.space

sentence: Human Tended Capability would be provided by the addition of the U.S. Common Module Module which adds subsystems and 9 payload racks and docking ports for ESA and Japanese laboratories.
label: sci.space

sentence: 60-day missions with the orbiter docked to the station are assumed for this phase.
label: sci.space

sentence: Different operation/utilization modes are being studied for this phase.
label: sci.space

sentence: Option B - Freedom Derived -- Mike Griffin presented the status of Option B activities.
label: sci.space

sentence: Griffin detailed the evolution of the Freedom-derived option, from initial Research Capability, to Human-Tended Capability, to Permanent Human Presence Capability, to Two Fault Tolerance, and finally Permanent Human Capability.
label: sci.space

sentence: Griffin also outlined proposed systems changes to the baseline program, with minor changes to the Communications and Tracking system, Crew Health Care System and ECLSS, and a major change to the Data Management System.
label: sci.space

sentence: Initial Research Capability would be achieved with 2 flights to 28.5 degree inclination (3 flights to 51.6 degrees) and consist of an extended duration orbiter-Spacelab combination docked to a truss segment with 2 photo voltaic arrays providing 18.75 kW of power.
label: sci.space

sentence: Human-Tended Capability would be achieved in 6 flights and add truss segments and the U.S. lab.
label: sci.space

sentence: Permanent Human Presence Capability would be achieved in 8 flights with two orbiters providing habitation and assured crew return.
label: sci.space

sentence: Two Fault Tolerance, achieved in 11 flights, would build out the other section of truss with another set of PV modules, thermal control and propulsion systems.
label: sci.space

sentence: The freedom derived configuration could achieve an International Complete state with 16 flights.
label: sci.space

sentence: Three more flights, to bring up the habitat module, a third PV array and two Assured Crew Return Vehicles (ACRV) would complete the Permanent Human Capability with International stage.
label: sci.space

sentence: Griffin told the Redesign Advisory Committee that eliminating hardware would not, by itself, meet budget guidelines for the Freedom derived option.
label: sci.space

sentence: Major reductions or deferrals must occur in other areas including program management, contractor non-hardware, early utilization and operations costs, he said.
label: sci.space

sentence: Option C - Singe Launch Core Station -- Chet Vaughn presented Option C, the Single Launch Core Station concept.
label: sci.space

sentence: A Shuttle external tank and solid rocket boosters would be used to launch the station into orbit.
label: sci.space

sentence: Shuttle main engines would be mounted to the tail of the station module for launch and jettisoned after ET separation.
label: sci.space

sentence: The module, 23 feet in diameter and 92 feet long, would provide 26,000 cubic feet of pressured volume, separated into 7 "decks" connected by a centralized passageway.
label: sci.space

sentence: Seven berthing ports would be located at various places on the circumference of the module to place the international modules, and other elements.
label: sci.space

sentence: This "can" would have two fixed photo voltaic arrays producing approximately 40 kW of power flying in a solar interial attitude.
label: sci.space

sentence: In his closing comments to the Redesign Advisory Committee, Bryan O'Connor said a design freeze would be established for the 3 options on April 26 so that detailed costing of the options can begin.
label: sci.space

sentence: The next meeting with the Redesign Advisory Committee will be May 3.
label: sci.space

sentence: Russian Consultants Arrive in U.S. -- A delegation of 16 Russian space experts arrived in the U.S. on April 21 and briefings to the SRT by members of the Russian team began on the 22nd.
label: sci.space

sentence: The group includes Russian Space Agency General Director Y. M. Koptev, and V. A. Yatsenko, also of the RSA.
label: sci.space

sentence: Others on the team include representatives from the Ministry of Defense, the Design Bureau SALYUT, the Institute of Biomedical Problems, the Ministry of Foreign Affairs, NPO Energia and TsNIJMASH.
label: sci.space

sentence: The Russian team briefed the SRT on environmental control and life support system, docking systems, the Proton launch vehicle, Mir operations and utilization, and the Soyuz TM spacecraft.
label: sci.space

sentence: The Russian consultants are available to the SRT to assess the capabilities of the Mir space station, and the possible use of Mir and other Russian capabilities and systems as part of the space station redesign.
label: sci.space

sentence: They will be available to the SRT through May 5.
label: sci.space

sentence: Management and Operations Review Continues -- Work continued in the SRT subgroups.
label: sci.space

sentence: The Management Group under Dr. Walt Brooks is working to develop a family of options that solve the current problems and build a foundation for the transition to development and operations.
label: sci.space

sentence: Various management options have been developed including: Lead Center with the Center Director in the programmatic chain of command.
label: sci.space

sentence: Host Center with the Program Manager reporting directly to an Associate Administrator.
label: sci.space

sentence: Skunk Works/Dedicated Program Office with a small dedicated co-located hand-picked program office.
label: sci.space

sentence: Combine Space Station with Shuttle, with the space station becoming an element of the current program.
label: sci.space

sentence: Major Tune Up to Current Organization, with current contracts and geographical distribution maintained but streamlined.
label: sci.space

sentence: The Operations Group under Dr. John Cox is building on the work of the Operations Phase Assessment Team lead by Gene Kranz of NASA-JSC, which had already begun a comprehensive review of operations and had concluded in its preliminary results that significant cost reductions are possible.
label: sci.space

sentence: As part of its work, the Operations Group has identified teams of agency experts to develop detailed evaluations of each design in the areas of assembly and operations, utilization, maintenance and logistics and testing and ground operations.
label: sci.space

sentence: What's in the Week Ahead?
label: sci.space

sentence: The Design Support Teams will provide a comprehensive status of their option to the Station Redesign Team on Monday and Tuesday at which point the design will be "frozen" to begin the detailed cost assessment.
label: sci.space

sentence: Also this week, the team will begin preparing for the next round of discussions with the redesign Advisory Committee, to be held May 3.
label: sci.space

sentence: Dr. Shea Steps Down -- Dr. Joe Shea stepped down as director of the Station Redesign Team on April 22 and Bryan O'Connor will take over the activities of the team.
label: sci.space

sentence: Dr. Shea submitted his resignation as assistant deputy administrator for space station analysis, but will continue to serve as a special advisory to NASA Administrator Goldin and be available to consult with the SRT.
label: sci.space

sentence: Mr Goldin accepted the resignation so that a request from Dr. Shea to reduce his workload could be accommodated.
label: sci.space

sentence: Key Milestones -- The key dates for the SRT as they are currently being carried on the schedule are: April 26 Design Freeze on Options for Costing April 27 Design Support Team Present Selected Options to SRT May 3
label: sci.space

sentence: Okay, lets get the record straight on the Livermore gas gun.
label: sci.space

sentence: The project manager is Dr. John Hunter, and he works for the Laser group at Livermore.
label: sci.space

sentence: What, you may ask, does gas guns have to do with lasers?
label: sci.space

sentence: Nothing, really, but the gun is physically located across the road from the Free Electron Laser building, and the FEL building has a heavily shielded control room (thick walls) from which the gun firings are controlled.
label: sci.space

sentence: So I suspect that the office he works for is an administrative convenience.
label: sci.space

sentence: I visited Hunter at the beginning of Feb. and we toured the gun.
label: sci.space

sentence: At the time I was working on gas gun R&D at Boeing, where I work, but I am now doing other things (helping to save the space station), The gun uses a methane-air mixture, which is burned in a chamber about 200 ft long by 16 inch ID (i.e. it looks like a pipe).
label: sci.space

sentence: The chamber holds a 1 ton piston which is propelled at several hundred m/s down the chamber.
label: sci.space

sentence: On the other side of the piston is hudrogen gas, initially at room temperature andsome tens of atmospheres.
label: sci.space

sentence: The piston compresses and heats the hydrogen ahead of it until a stainless steel burst diaphragm ruptures, at around 50,000 psi.
label: sci.space

sentence: The barrel of the gun is about 100 feet long and has a 4 inch bore.
label: sci.space

sentence: It is mounted at right angles to the chamber (i.e. they intersect).
label: sci.space

sentence: This was done so that in the future, the barrel could be raised and the gun fired into the air without having to move the larger and heavier chamber.
label: sci.space

sentence: The projectile being used in testing is a 5 kg cylinder of Lexan plastic, 4 in in diameter and about 50 cm long.
label: sci.space

sentence: All of the acceleration comes from the expansion of the hydrogen gas from 50,000 psi downwards until the projectile leaves the barrel.
label: sci.space

sentence: The barrel is evacuated, and the end is sealed with a sheet of plastic film (a little thicker than Saran wrap).
label: sci.space

sentence: The plastic is blown off by the small amount of residual air trapped in the barrel ahead of the projectile.
label: sci.space

sentence: The gun is fired into a bunker filled with sandbags and plastic water jugs.
label: sci.space

sentence: In the early testing fragments of the plastic projectile were found.
label: sci.space

sentence: At the higher speeds in later testing, the projectile vaporizes.
label: sci.space

sentence: The testing is into a bunker because the Livermore test range is about 3 miles across, and the projectile would go 100-200 km if fired for maximum range.
label: sci.space

sentence: The intent is to move the whole gun to Vandenberg AFB after the testing is complete, where they can fire into the Pacific Ocean, and use the tracking radar at VAFB to follow the projectiles.
label: sci.space

sentence: The design goal of the gun is to throw a 5 kg projectile at 4 km/s (half of orbital speed).
label: sci.space

sentence: So far they have reached 2 km/s, and the gun is currently down for repairs, as on the last test they blew a seal and damaged some of the hardware (I think it had to do with the methane-air more detonating than burning, but I haven't had a chance to talk to Hunter directly on this).
label: sci.space

sentence: There are people waiting to test scramjet components in this gun by firing then out of the gun into the air (at Mach 12= 4 km/s), since the most you can get in wind tunnels is Mach 8.

sentence: Gibbons Outlines Space Station Redesign Guidance Dr. John H. Gibbons, Director, Office of Science and Technology Policy, outlined to the members-designate of the Advisory Committee on the Redesign of the Space Station on April 3, three budget options as guidance to the committee in their deliberations on the redesign of the space station.
label: sci.space

sentence: A low option of $5 billion, a mid-range option of $7 billion and a high option of $9 billion will be considered by the committee.
label: sci.space

sentence: Each option would cover the total expenditures for space station from fiscal year 1994 through 1998 and would include funds for development, operations, utilization, Shuttle integration, facilities, research operations support, transition cost and also must include adequate program reserves to insure program implementation within the available funds.
label: sci.space

sentence: Over the next 5 years, $4 billion is reserved within the NASA budget for the President's new technology investment.
label: sci.space

sentence: As a result, station options above $7 billion must be accompanied by offsetting reductions in the rest of the NASA budget.
label: sci.space

sentence: For example, a space station option of $9 billion would require $2 billion in offsets from the NASA budget over the next 5 years.
label: sci.space

sentence: Gibbons presented the information at an organizational session of the advisory committee.
label: sci.space

sentence: Generally, the members-designate focused upon administrative topics and used the session to get acquainted.
label: sci.space

sentence: They also received a legal and ethics briefing and an orientation on the process the Station Redesign Team is following to develop options for the advisory committee to consider.
label: sci.space

sentence: Gibbons also announced that the United States and its international partners -- the Europeans, Japanese, and Canadians -- have decided, after consultation, to give "full consideration" to use of Russian assets in the course of the space station redesign process.
label: sci.space

sentence: To that end, the Russians will be asked to participate in the redesign effort on an as-needed consulting basis, so that the redesign team can make use of their expertise in assessing the capabilities of MIR and the possible use of MIR and other Russian capabilities and systems.
label: sci.space

sentence: The U.S. and international partners hope to benefit from the expertise of the Russian participants in assessing Russian systems and technology.
label: sci.space

sentence: The overall goal of the redesign effort is to develop options for reducing station costs while preserving key research and exploration capabilities.
label: sci.space

sentence: Careful integration of Russian assets could be a key factor in achieving that goal.
label: sci.space

sentence: Gibbons reiterated that, "President Clinton is committed to the redesigned space station and to making every effort to preserve the science, the technology and the jobs that the space station program represents.
label: sci.space

sentence: However, he also is committed to a space station that is well managed and one that does not consume the national resources which should be used to invest in the future of this industry and this nation."

sentence: On June 2 and 7, 1983, two of the Soviet Union's mighty PROTON rockets lifted off from the Tyuratam Space Center in the Kazakhstan Republic.
label: sci.space

sentence: Aboard those boosters were a new breed of VENERA probe for the planet Venus.
label: sci.space

sentence: Designated VENERA 15 and 16, the probes were meant not for landing yet more spherical craft on the Venerean surface but to radar map the planet in detail from orbit.
label: sci.space

sentence: To accomplish this task, the basic VENERA design was modified in numerous areas.
label: sci.space

sentence: The central bus core was made one meter (39.37 inches) longer to carry the two tons of propellant required for braking into orbit, double the fuel carried by the VENERA 9 and 10 orbiters eight years earlier.
label: sci.space

sentence: Extra solar panels were added on to give the vehicles more power for handling the large amounts of data which would be created by the radar imaging.
label: sci.space

sentence: The dish-shaped communications antennae were also made one meter larger to properly transmit this information to Earth.
label: sci.space

sentence: Atop the buses, where landers were usually placed, were installed the 1.4 by 6-meter (4.62 by 19.8-foot), 300-kilogram (660-pound) POLYUS V side-looking radar antennae.
label: sci.space

sentence: The radar system, possibly a terrain-imaging version of the nuclear-powered satellites used by the Soviets for Earth ocean surveillance, would be able to map Venus' surface at a resolution of one to two kilometers (0.62 to 1.2 miles).
label: sci.space

sentence: The Soviet probes' imaging parameters were a vast improvement over the United States PIONEER VENUS Orbiter, which could reveal objects no smaller than 75 kilometers (45 miles) in diameter.
label: sci.space

sentence: And while the VENERAs' resolution was comparable to that of similar observations made by the 300-meter (1,000-foot) Arecibo radio telescope on the island of Puerto Rico, the orbiters would be examining the northern pole of Venus.
label: sci.space

sentence: This region was unobtainable by either Arecibo or PIONEER VENUS and appeared to contain a number of potentially interesting geological features worthy of investigation.
label: sci.space

sentence: On October 10, 1983, after an interplanetary journey of 330 million kilometers (198 million miles) and two mid-course corrections, VENERA 15 fired its braking rockets over Venus to place itself in a polar orbit 1,000 by 65,000 kilometers (600 by 39,000 miles) around the planet, completing one revolution every twenty-four hours.
label: sci.space

sentence: VENERA 16 followed suit four days later.
label: sci.space

sentence: The twin probes thus became Venus' first polar-circling spacecraft.
label: sci.space

sentence: Radar operations began on October 16 for VENERA 15 and October 20 for VENERA 16.
label: sci.space

sentence: For up to sixteen minutes every orbit over the north pole, the probes would make a radar sweep of the surface 150 kilometers (ninety miles) wide and nine thousand kilometers (5,400 miles) long.
label: sci.space

sentence: The craft would then head out to the highest part of their orbits over the south pole to recharge their batteries and transmit the data back to two large Soviet antennae on Earth.
label: sci.space

sentence: Each strip of information took eight hours to process by computer.
label: sci.space

sentence: By the end of their main missions in July of 1984, the VENERAs had mapped 115 million square kilometers (46 million square miles), thirty percent of the entire planet.
label: sci.space

sentence: VENERA 15 and 16 revealed that Venus has a surface geology more complex than shown by PIONEER VENUS in the late 1970s.
label: sci.space

sentence: Numerous hills, mountains, ridges, valleys, and plains spread across the landscape, many of them apparently formed by lava from erupting volcanoes in the last one billion years.
label: sci.space

sentence: In planetary terms this makes the Venerean surface rather young.
label: sci.space

sentence: Hundreds of craters were detected as well, the largest of which had to have been created by meteorites (planetoids would be a better term here) at least fourteen kilometers (8.4 miles) across, due to Venus' very dense atmosphere.
label: sci.space

sentence: There were some disagreements between U.S. and Soviet scientists on the origins of certain surface features.
label: sci.space

sentence: For example, the probes' owners declared that the 96-kilometer (57.6-mile) wide crater at the summit of 10,800-meter (35,640-foot) high Maxwell Montes, the tallest mountain on the planet, was the result of a meteorite impact.
label: sci.space

sentence: American scientists, on the other hand, felt the crater was proof that Maxwell was a huge volcano sitting on the northern "continent" of Ishtar Terra.
label: sci.space

sentence: In any event, the U.S. decided to wait on making verdicts about Venus until the arrival of their own radar probe, scheduled for later in the decade.
label: sci.space

sentence: Originally named the Venus Orbiting Imaging Radar (VOIR), its initial design was scaled back and the craft was redesig- nated the Venus Radar Mapper (VRM).
label: sci.space

sentence: Eventually the machine would be called MAGELLAN, after the Portuguese navigator Ferdinand Magellan (circa 1480-1521).
label: sci.space

sentence: This vehicle would map the entire planet in even finer detail than the VENERAs.
label: sci.space

sentence: For the time, however, the Soviet probes maintained that distinction.
label: sci.space

sentence: Radar imaging was not the only ability of the VENERAs.
label: sci.space

sentence: Bolted next to the POLYUS V radar antenna were the Omega altimeter and the Fourier infrared spectrometer, the latter for measuring the world's temperatures.
label: sci.space

sentence: The majority of the areas covered registered about five hundred degrees Celsius (932 degrees Fahrenheit), but a few locations were two hundred degrees hotter, possibly indicating current volcanic activity.
label: sci.space

sentence: The probes also found that the clouds over the poles were five to eight kilometers (three to 4.8 miles) lower than at the equator.
label: sci.space

sentence: In contrast, the polar air above sixty kilometers (thirty-six miles) altitude was five to twenty degrees warmer than the equatorial atmosphere at similar heights.
label: sci.space

sentence: When the main mapping mission ended in July of 1984, there were plans for at least one of the VENERAs to radar image the surface at more southernly latitudes.
label: sci.space

sentence: Unfortunately this idea did not come to pass, as the orbiters may not have possessed enough attitude-control gas to perform the operation.
label: sci.space

sentence: VENERA 15 and 16 ceased transmission in March of 1985, leaving the Soviet Institute of Radiotechnology and Electronics with six hundred kilometers (360 miles) of radar data tape to sort into an atlas of twenty-seven maps of the northern hemisphere of Venus.
label: sci.space

sentence: Venus by Balloon For years the thick atmosphere of Venus had been a tempting target to scientists who wished to explore the planet's mantle of air with balloon-borne instruments.
label: sci.space

sentence: Professor Jacques Blamont of the French space agency Centre National d'Etudes Spatiales (CNES) had proposed such an idea as far back as 1967, only to have a joint French-Soviet balloon mission canceled in 1982.
label: sci.space

sentence: Nevertheless, late in the year 1984, such dreams would eventually come true.
label: sci.space

sentence: When two PROTON rockets were sent skyward on December 15 and 21, the Soviet Union provided Western observers with the first clear, full views of the booster which had been launching every Soviet Venus probe since 1975.
label: sci.space

sentence: This was but one of many firsts for the complex mission.
label: sci.space

sentence: The unmanned probes launched into space that December were named VEGA 1 and 2, a contraction of the words VENERA and GALLEI - Gallei being the Russian word for Halley.
label: sci.space

sentence: Not only did the spacecraft have more than one mission to perform, they also had more than one celestial objective to explore, namely the comet Halley.
label: sci.space

sentence: This famous periodic traveler was making its latest return to the inner regions of the solar system since its last visit in 1910.
label: sci.space

sentence: Since it was widely believed that comets are the icy remains from the formation of the solar system five billion years ago, scientists around the world gave high priority to exploring one of the few such bodies which actually come close to Earth.
label: sci.space

sentence: Most comets linger in the cold and dark outer fringes of the solar system.
label: sci.space

sentence: Some, like Halley, are perturbed by various forces and fall in towards the Sun, where they circle for millennia spewing out ice and debris for millions of kilometers from the warmth of each solar encounter.
label: sci.space

sentence: The Soviet Union, along with the European Space Agency (ESA) and Japan's Institute of Space and Astronautical Science (ISAS), did not wish to miss out on this first opportunity in human history to make a close examination of Halley.
label: sci.space

sentence: The ESA would be using the cylindrical GIOTTO probe to make a dangerously close photographic flyby of the comet, while Japan's first deep space craft - SAKIGAKE (Pioneer) and SUISEI (Comet) - would view Halley from a much safer distance.
label: sci.space

sentence: Scientists in the United States also desired to study the comet from the vantage of a space probe, at one time envisioning a vessel powered by solar sails or ion engines.
label: sci.space

sentence: However, government budget cuts to NASA canceled the American efforts.
label: sci.space

sentence: The U.S. would have to make do primarily with Earth-based observations and the sharing of data from other nations, though an instrument named the Dust Counter and Mass Analyzer (DUCMA), designed by Chicago University Professor John Simpson, was added on the Soviet mission in May of 1984.
label: sci.space

sentence: The Soviets' answer to Halley were the VEGAs.
label: sci.space

sentence: Instead of building an entirely new craft for the mission, the Soviets decided to modify their VENERA bus design to encounter the comet while performing an advanced Venus mission along the way.
label: sci.space

sentence: As VEGA 1 and 2 reached Venus, the buses would drop off one lander/balloon each and use the mass of the shrouded planet to swing them towards comet Halley, much as the U.S. probe MARINER 10 used Venus to flyby Mercury eleven years earlier.
label: sci.space

sentence: The Soviet craft would then head on to Halley, helping to pinpoint the location of the comet's erupting nucleus for the GIOTTO probe to dive in only 605 kilometers (363 miles) away in March of 1986.
label: sci.space

sentence: As planned, the two VEGAs arrived at Venus in June of 1985.
label: sci.space

sentence: VEGA 1 released its payload first on the ninth day of the month, the lander making a two-day descent towards the planet.
label: sci.space

sentence: The craft touched the upper atmosphere on the morning of June 11.
label: sci.space

sentence: Sixty-one kilometers (36.6 miles) above the Venerean surface a small container was released by the lander, which produced a parachute at 55 kilometers (33 miles) altitude.
label: sci.space

sentence: Thus the first balloon probe ever to explore Venus had successfully arrived.
label: sci.space

sentence: One kilometer after the opening of the parachute, helium gas was pumped into the Teflon-coated plastic balloon, inflating it to a diameter of 3.54 meters (11.68 feet).
label: sci.space

sentence: Dangling on a tether thirteen meters (42.9 feet) below was the instrument package, properly known as an aerostat.
label: sci.space

sentence: The top part of the 6.9-kilogram (15.18-pound) aerostat consisted of a cone which served as an antenna and tether attachment point to the balloon.
label: sci.space

sentence: Beneath it was the transmitter, electronics, and instruments.
label: sci.space

sentence: Connected at the bottom was a nephelometer for measuring cloud particles.
label: sci.space

sentence: The aerostat was painted with a special white finish to keep at bay the corroding mist of sulfuric acid which permeated the planet's atmosphere.
label: sci.space

sentence: The VEGA 1 balloon was dropped into the night side of Venus just north of the equator.
label: sci.space

sentence: Scientists were concerned that the gas bag would burst in the heat of daylight, so they placed it in the darkened hemisphere to give the craft as much time as possible to return data.
label: sci.space

sentence: This action necessitated that the landers come down in the dark as well, effectively removing the camera systems used on previous missions.
label: sci.space

sentence: The author wonders, though, if they could have used floodlights similar to the ones attached to VENERA 9 and 10 in 1975, when Soviet scientists had thought the planet's surface was enshrouded in a perpetual twilight due to the permanently thick cloud cover.
label: sci.space

sentence: The first balloon transmitted for 46.5 hours right into the day hemisphere before its lithium batteries failed, covering 11,600 kilometers (6,960 miles).
label: sci.space

sentence: The threat of bursting in the day heat did not materialize.
label: sci.space

sentence: The VEGA 1 balloon was stationed at a 54-kilometer (32.4-mile) altitude after dropping ballast at fifty kilometers (thirty miles), for this was considered the most active of the three main cloud layers reported by PIONEER VENUS in 1978.
label: sci.space

sentence: Indeed the balloon was pushed across the planet at speeds up to 250 kilometers (150 miles) per hour.
label: sci.space

sentence: Strong vertical winds bobbed the craft up and down two to three hundred meters (660 to 990 feet) through most of the journey.
label: sci.space

sentence: The layer's air temperature averaged forty degrees Celsius (104 degrees Fahrenheit) and pressure was a mere 0.5 Earth atmosphere.
label: sci.space

sentence: The nephelometer could find no clear regions in the surrounding clouds.
label: sci.space

sentence: Early in the first balloon's flight, the VEGA 1 lander was already headed towards the Venerean surface.
label: sci.space

sentence: Both landers were equipped with a soil drill and analyzer similar to the ones carried on VENERA 13 and 14 in 1982.
label: sci.space

sentence: However, VEGA 1 would become unable to report the composition of the ground at its landing site in Rusalka Planitia, the Mermaid Plain north of Aphrodite Terra.
label: sci.space

sentence: While still ten to fifteen minutes away from landing, a timer malfunction caused the drill to accidentally begin its programmed activity sixteen kilometers (9.6 miles) above the surface.
label: sci.space

sentence: There was neither any way to shut off the instrument before touchdown nor reactivate it after landing.
label: sci.space

sentence: This was unfortunate not only for the general loss of data but also for the fact that most of Venus was covered with such smooth low-level lava plains and had never before been directly examined.
label: sci.space

sentence: Nevertheless, the surface temperature and pressure was calculated at 468 degrees Celsius (874.4 degrees Fahrenheit) and 95 Earth atmospheres respectively during the lander's 56 minutes of ground transmissions.
label: sci.space

sentence: A large amount of background infrared radiation was also recorded at the site.
label: sci.space

sentence: As had been done when the drills and cameras on VENERA 11 and 12 had failed in December of 1978, the Soviets focused on the data returned during the lander's plunge through the atmosphere.
label: sci.space

sentence: The French-Soviet Malachite mass spectrometer detected sulfur, chlorine, and possibly phosphorus.
label: sci.space

sentence: It is the sulfur - possibly from active volcanoes - which gives the Venerean clouds their yellowish color.
label: sci.space

sentence: The Sigma 3 gas chromatograph found that every cubic meter of air between an altitude of 48 and 63 kilometers (28.8 and 37.8 miles) contained one milligram (0.015 grain) of sulfuric acid.
label: sci.space

sentence: The VEGA 1 data on the overall structure of the cloud decks appeared to be at odds with the information from PIONEER VENUS.
label: sci.space

sentence: The case was made even stronger by the fact that VEGA 2's results nearly matched its twin.
label: sci.space

sentence: The VEGAs found only two main cloud layers instead of the three reported by the U.S. probes.
label: sci.space

sentence: The layers were three to five kilometers (1.8 to 3 miles) thick at altitudes of 50 and 58 kilometers (30 and 34.8 miles).
label: sci.space

sentence: The clouds persisted like a thin fog until clearing at an altitude of 35 kilometers (21 miles), much lower than the PV readings.
label: sci.space

sentence: One possibility for the discrepan- cies may have been radical structural changes in the Venerean air over the last seven years.
label: sci.space

sentence: When the lander and balloon finally went silent, the last functioning part of the VEGA 1 mission, the flyby bus, sailed on for a 708 million-kilometer (424.8 million-mile) journey around the Sun to become the first probe to meet comet Halley.
label: sci.space

sentence: On March 6, 1986, the bus made a 8,890-kilometer (5,334-mile) pass at the dark and icy visitor before traveling on in interplanetary space.
label: sci.space

sentence: The Soviets had accomplished their first mission to two celestial bodies with one space vessel.
label: sci.space

sentence: On June 13, VEGA 2 released its lander/balloon payload for a two-day fall towards Venus.
label: sci.space

sentence: Like its duplicate, the VEGA 2 balloon radioed information back to the twenty antennae tracking it on Earth for 46.5 hours before battery failure on the morning side of the planet.
label: sci.space

sentence: During its 11,100-kilometer (6,660-mile) flight over Venus, the second balloon entered in a rather still environment which became less so twenty hours into the mission.
label: sci.space

sentence: After 33 hours mission time the air became even more turbulent for a further eight hours.
label: sci.space

sentence: When the balloon passed over a five- kilometer (three-mile) mountain on the "continent" of Aphrodite Terra, a powerful downdraft pulled the craft 2.5 kilometers (1.5 miles) towards the surface.
label: sci.space

sentence: Temperature sensors on the VEGA 2 balloon reported that the air layer it was moving through was consistently 6.5 degrees Celsius (43.7 degrees Fahrenheit) cooler than the area explored by the VEGA 1 balloon.
label: sci.space

sentence: This was corroborated by the VEGA 2 lander as it passed through the balloon's level.
label: sci.space

sentence: No positive indications of lightning were made by either balloon, and the second aerostat's nephelometer failed to function.
label: sci.space

sentence: The VEGA 2 lander touched down on the northern edge of Aphrodite Terra's western arm on the fifteenth of June, 1,500 kilometers (900 miles) southeast of VEGA 1.
label: sci.space

sentence: The lander's resting place was smoother than thought, indicating either a very ancient and worn surface or a relatively young one covered in fresh lava.
label: sci.space

sentence: The soil drill was in working order and reported a rock type known as anorthosite-troctolite, rare on Earth but present in Luna's highlands.
label: sci.space

sentence: This rock is rich in aluminum and silicon but lacking in iron and magnesium.
label: sci.space

sentence: A high degree of sulfur was also present in the soil.
label: sci.space

sentence: The air around VEGA 2 measured 463 degrees Celsius (865.4 degrees Fahrenheit) and 91 Earth atmospheres, essentially a typical day (or night) on Venus.
label: sci.space

sentence: Far above the VEGA 2 lander, its carrier bus sped past Venus at a distance of 24,500 kilometers (14,700 miles) and followed its twin to comet Halley, making a closer flyby on March 9, 1986 at just 8,030 kilometers (4,818 miles).
label: sci.space

sentence: Both probes helped to reveal that the comet is a very dark and irregular-shaped mass about fourteen kilometers (8.4 miles) across, rotating once every 53 hours, give or take three hours.
label: sci.space

sentence: Since both VEGA craft were still functioning after their Halley encounters, Soviet scientists considered an option to send the probes to other celestial objects.
label: sci.space

sentence: One prime target was the near- Earth planetoid 2101 Adonis, which VEGA 2 could pass at a distance of six million kilometers (3.6 million miles).
label: sci.space

sentence: Sadly, the Soviets had to back out on the opportunity to become the first nation to fly a spacecraft past a planetoid when it was discovered that there was not enough maneuvering fuel in the probe to reach Adonis as planned.
label: sci.space

sentence: VEGA 1 and 2 were quietly shut down in early 1987.
label: sci.space

sentence: Future Plans Diverted The impressive VEGA mission had given some scientists numerous ideas and hope for even more ambitious expeditions to the second world from the Sun.
label: sci.space

sentence: One example was the VESTA mission, planned for the early 1990s.
label: sci.space

sentence: This Soviet-French collaboration called for the launch of multiple probes on a single PROTON rocket in either 1991 or 1992.
label: sci.space

sentence: The craft would first swing by Venus and drop off several landers and balloon probes.
label: sci.space

sentence: The aerostats would be designed to survive in the planet's corrosive atmosphere for up to one month, a large improvement over the VEGA balloons' two days.
label: sci.space

sentence: The mission would then head out to investigate several planetoids and comets, including a possible landing on Vesta (thus the mission name), the most reflective Main Belt planetoid as seen from Earth.
label: sci.space

sentence: Unfortunately for Venus exploration, plans began to change in the Soviet Union.
label: sci.space

sentence: In 1986 the Soviets decided to reroute the VESTA mission to the red planet Mars instead of Venus, keeping the comet and planetoid aspects intact.
label: sci.space

sentence: By this time in the Soviet space program interest was focusing on Mars.
label: sci.space

sentence: Already under construction was an entirely new probe design called PHOBOS.
label: sci.space

sentence: Two members of this class were planned to leave Earth in 1988 and orbit Mars the next year.
label: sci.space

sentence: PHOBOS 1 and 2 would then place the first instruments on Mars' largest moon, Phobos.
label: sci.space

sentence: All this was a prelude to even more advanced Mars expeditions, including balloon probes, rovers, soil sample return craft, and eventually human explorers in the early Twenty-First Century.
label: sci.space

sentence: The environment of Venus was just too hostile for any serious consideration of human colonization in the near future.
label: sci.space

sentence: But things began to look bleak for Soviet Venus and Mars exploration.
label: sci.space

sentence: Both PHOBOS probes failed to complete their missions, one losing contact on the way to the Red Planet in 1988 and the other going silent in Mars orbit just one week before the planned moon landing in March of 1989.
label: sci.space

sentence: In 1989 a plan was devised for a Venus orbiter to drop eight to ten penetrators around the planet in 1998.
label: sci.space

sentence: Several years later the mission launch date was moved to the year 2005 and has now been put on indefinite hold.
label: sci.space

sentence: No other official Soviet missions to Venus have since been put forth, a sad commentary after twenty-five years of continuous robotic exploration of the planet.
label: sci.space

sentence: During the late 1980s a drastic political and economic change was taking over the Soviet Union.
label: sci.space

sentence: President Mikhail Gorbachev began to "open up" his nation to the benefits of increased cooperation with the rest of the nations, particularly those in the West.
label: sci.space

sentence: While the culture became less oppressive than in the past, the economy was taking a very rough ride as it also underwent the effects of a "free market".
label: sci.space

sentence: These effects hit everywhere, including the space program.
label: sci.space

sentence: Missions at all levels were cut back.
label: sci.space

sentence: The Soviets began making almost desperate attempts to cooperate with other space-faring nations either to keep their remaining programs alive or just to make money.
label: sci.space

sentence: In early 1992 it was reported that the Soviets were offering for sale several fully-equipped VENERAs they had in storage for the price of 1.6 million dollars each, an incredibly low price for any planetary probe.
label: sci.space

sentence: No nation took them up on the bargain.
label: sci.space

sentence: Meanwhile the United States was gearing up for new Venus missions of their own.
label: sci.space

sentence: MAGELLAN and GALILEO
label: sci.space

sentence: The U.S. reactivated their long-dormant planetary exploration with the launch of the Space Shuttle ATLANTIS on May 4, 1989.
label: sci.space

sentence: Aboard the Shuttle was the MAGELLAN spacecraft, a combination of spare parts from other U.S. probes designed to make the most detailed and complete radar-mapping of Venus in history.
label: sci.space

sentence: When MAGELLAN reached the second world in August of 1990, it would be able to map almost the entire planet down to a resolution of 108 meters (360 feet), surpassing the abilities of VENERA 15 and 16.
label: sci.space

sentence: In the interim another American probe was launched from a Space Shuttle which would make a quick flyby of Venus on its way to orbit the giant planet Jupiter in 1995.
label: sci.space

sentence: On October 18, 1989, the Shuttle ATLANTIS released its second unmanned planetary probe into space, named GALILEO after the famous Italian astronomer who discovered the probe's primary target's major moons in 1610.
label: sci.space

sentence: In the absence of a powerful enough booster to send GALILEO on a direct flight to the Jovian planet, the probe was sent around Venus and Earth several times to build up enough speed to reach Jupiter.
label: sci.space

sentence: As a result, Venus became GALILEO's first planetary goal in February of 1990.
label: sci.space

sentence: The probe radioed back images of the planet's swirling clouds and further indications of lightning in that violent atmosphere.
label: sci.space

sentence: On the Drawing Boards With the incredible success of MAGELLAN in the last few years, new plans have been laid out for further journeys to Venus. Scien-
label: sci.space

sentence: tists in the U.S. have talked to space scientists in the former Soviet Union - now the Commonwealth of Independent States since January 1, 1992 - of a cooperative effort to launch new VENERA lander missions within in the next decade.
label: sci.space

sentence: Japan, India, and the ESA have also considered their own Venus missions in the next few decades.
label: sci.space

sentence: In February of 1993 NASA came up with several new Venus projects as part of their Discovery Program for launching inexpensive probes throughout the solar system.
label: sci.space

sentence: For Venus two missions were selected for further study: A Venus Multiprobe Mission involving the landing of fourteen small probes over one hemisphere to measure winds, air temperature, and pressure; and the Venus Composition Probe, designed to study Venus' atmosphere while descending through the thick air with the aid of a parachute, much as the Soviets had done since 1967.
label: sci.space

sentence: Final project decisions will be made in 1994.
label: sci.space

sentence: Humans on Venus Will a human ever be able to stand on the surface of Venus?
label: sci.space

sentence: At present the lead-melting temperatures and crushing air pressure would be threatening to any Earth life not protected in something even tougher than a VENERA lander.
label: sci.space

sentence: Plans have been looked into changing the environment of Venus itself into something more like Earth's.
label: sci.space

sentence: However, it should be noted that any such undertaking will require the removal of much of the thick carbon dioxide atmosphere, a major reduction in surface heat, and the ability to speed up the planet's rotation rate to something a bit faster than once every 243 Earth days.
label: sci.space

sentence: Such a project may take centuries if not millennia.
label: sci.space

sentence: In the meantime efforts should be made to better understand Venus as its exists today.
label: sci.space

sentence: We still have yet to fully know how a world so seemingly similar to Earth in many important ways became instead such a deadly place.
label: sci.space

sentence: Will Earth ever suffer this fate?
label: sci.space

sentence: Perhaps Venus holds the answers.
label: sci.space

sentence: Such answers may best be found through international cooperation, including the nation which made the first attempts to lift the cloudy veils from Venus.
label: sci.space

sentence: WHAT HAPPENED TO THE SATURN V PLANS Despite a widespread belief to the contrary, the Saturn V blueprints have not been lost.
label: sci.space

sentence: They are kept at Marshall Space Flight Center on microfilm.
label: sci.space

sentence: The problem in re-creating the Saturn V is not finding the drawings, it is finding vendors who can supply mid-1960's vintage hardware (like guidance system components), and the fact that the launch pads and VAB have been converted to Space Shuttle use, so you have no place to launch from.
label: sci.space

sentence: By the time you redesign to accommodate available hardware and re-modify the launch pads, you may as well have started from scratch with a clean sheet design.
label: sci.space

sentence: WHY DATA FROM SPACE MISSIONS ISN'T IMMEDIATELY AVAILABLE Investigators associated with NASA missions are allowed exclusive access for one year after the data is obtained in order to give them an opportunity to analyze the data and publish results without being "scooped" by people uninvolved in the mission.
label: sci.space

sentence: However, NASA frequently releases examples (in non-digital form, e.g. photos) to the public early in a mission.

sentence: (2) In April 1970, the Apollo 13 lunar module reentered the atmosphere and its SNAP 27 RTG heat source, which was jettisoned, fell intact into the 20,000 feet deep Tonga Trench in the Pacific Ocean.

sentence: IMPACT OF THE SPACE SHUTTLE ON THE OZONE LAYER
label: sci.space

sentence: From time to time, claims are made that chemicals released from the Space Shuttle's Solid Rocket Boosters (SRBs) are responsible for a significant amount of damage to the ozone layer.

sentence: Studies indicate that they in reality have only a minute impact, both in absolute terms and relative to other chemical sources.

sentence: The atmospheric modelling study of the space shuttle effects on the stratosphere involved three independent theoretical groups, and was organized by Dr. Michael Prather, NASA/Goddard Institute for Space Studies.
label: sci.space

sentence: The three groups involved Michael Prather and Maria Garcia (NASA/GISS), Charlie Jackman and Anne Douglass (NASA/Goddard Space Flight Center), and Malcolm Ko and Dak Sze (Atmospheric and Environmental Research, Inc.).
label: sci.space

sentence: The effort was to look at the effects of the space shuttle and Titan rockets on the stratosphere.
label: sci.space

sentence: The following are the estimated sources of stratospheric chlorine: Industrial sources: 300,000,000 kilograms/year Natural sources: 75,000,000 kilograms/year Shuttle sources: 725,000 kilograms/year
label: sci.space

sentence: The shuttle source assumes 9 space shuttles and 6 Titan rockets are launched yearly.
label: sci.space

sentence: Thus the launches would add less than 0.25% to the total stratospheric chlorine sources.
label: sci.space

sentence: The effect on ozone is minimal: global yearly average total ozone would be decreased by 0.0065%.
label: sci.space

sentence: This is much less than total ozone variability associated with volcanic activity and solar flares.
label: sci.space

sentence: The influence of human-made chlorine products on ozone is computed by atmospheric model calculations to be a 1% decrease in globally averaged ozone between 1980 and 1990.
label: sci.space

sentence: The influence of the space shuttle and Titan rockets on the stratosphere is negligible.
label: sci.space

sentence: The launch schedule of the Space Shuttle and Titan rockets would need to be increased by over a factor of a hundred in order to have about the same effect on ozone as our increases in industrial halocarbons do at the present time.

sentence: HOW LONG CAN A HUMAN LIVE UNPROTECTED IN SPACE If you *don't* try to hold your breath, exposure to space for half a minute or so is unlikely to produce permanent injury.
label: sci.space

sentence: Holding your breath is likely to damage your lungs, something scuba divers have to watch out for when ascending, and you'll have eardrum trouble if your Eustachian tubes are badly plugged up, but theory predicts -- and animal experiments confirm -- that otherwise, exposure to vacuum causes no immediate injury.

sentence: HOW THE CHALLENGER ASTRONAUTS DIED The Challenger shuttle launch was not destroyed in an explosion.
label: sci.space

sentence: This is a well-documented fact; see the Rogers Commission report, for example.
label: sci.space

sentence: What looked like an explosion was fuel burning after the external tank came apart.

sentence: USING THE SHUTTLE BEYOND LOW EARTH ORBIT You can't use the shuttle orbiter for missions beyond low Earth orbit because it can't get there.
label: sci.space

sentence: It is big and heavy and does not carry enough fuel, even if you fill part of the cargo bay with tanks.
label: sci.space

sentence: Furthermore, it is not particularly sensible to do so, because much of that weight is things like wings, which are totally useless except in the immediate vicinity of the Earth.
label: sci.space

sentence: The shuttle orbiter is highly specialized for travel between Earth's surface and low orbit.
label: sci.space

sentence: Taking it higher is enormously costly and wasteful.
label: sci.space

sentence: A much better approach would be to use shuttle subsystems to build a specialized high-orbit spacecraft.

label: sci.space

sentence: There really is a big rock on Mars that looks remarkably like a humanoid face.
label: sci.space

sentence: It appears in two different frames of Viking Orbiter imagery: 35A72 (much more facelike in appearance, and the one more often published, with the Sun 10 degrees above western horizon) and 70A13 (with the Sun 27 degrees from the west).

sentence: X-Sender: newtout 0.08 Feb 23 1993 Introducing the Back to the Moon in Congress: The Next Step The next key hurdle for the Lunar Resources Data Purchase Act is introduction of the Act in Congress.
label: sci.space

sentence: At this point, many congresspersons have been approached about the bill.
label: sci.space

sentence: However, for a successful effort to pass the bill, we need the best possible congressperson to introduce the bill.
label: sci.space

sentence: Due to his position as Chair of the House Committee on Space and Science, Congressman George Brown is the logical choice.
label: sci.space

sentence: He has a long record of support and interest in space development, and helped pass the Launch Services Purchase Act and the Space Settlements Act.
label: sci.space

sentence: There is a small group of activists in southern California who have assisted George Brown in his recent re-election campaigns.
label: sci.space

sentence: We are mobilizing this group to have them tell Congressman Brown about the Back to the Moon bill.
label: sci.space

sentence: We are also asking pro-space constituents to let him know that they care about getting America back to the Moon.
label: sci.space

sentence: Finally, there is a good chance that a nationwide alert for space activists to call or write George Brown to have him introduce the Back to the Moon bill may be staged during late spring, 1993.
label: sci.space

sentence: All this should produce a positive reaction from Brown's office.
label: sci.space

sentence: As more is known, it will be passed on.
label: sci.space

sentence: However, even if we are successful in getting him to support the bill, this alone will not ensure passage of the bill.
label: sci.space

sentence: For any bill to become law, one of three conditions must exist: either the bill must reflect widespread national support for an issue (such as extension of unemployment insurance benefits); be propelled by high-priced lobbyists (we're out of luck there); or have widespread support within Congress, due to small, but widespread, constituent support.
label: sci.space

sentence: The latter is the path that we, by necessity, must choose.
label: sci.space

sentence: This means that the introduction of the Lunar Resources Data Purchase Act must be immediately accompanied by a large number of congresspersons' sponsorship of the bill.
label: sci.space

sentence: To accomplish this, we need activists to ask their congressperson to support the Lunar Resources Data Purchase Act - now.
label: sci.space

sentence: To wait until the bill is introduced is simply too late - it takes time to have a congressperson's staff review a bill.

sentence: If your congressperson mentions that the bill is not yet introduced, please elicit their opinion of the bill as currently written.

sentence: We appreciate all comments on the bill from activists and politicians.

sentence: WHY DOES THE SHUTTLE ROLL JUST AFTER LIFTOFF?
label: sci.space

sentence: The following answer and translation are provided by Ken Jenks (kjenks@gothamcity.jsc.nasa.gov).
label: sci.space

sentence: The "Ascent Guidance and Flight Control Training Manual," ASC G&C 2102, says: "During the vertical rise phase, the launch pad attitude is commanded until an I-loaded V(rel) sufficient to assure launch tower clearance is achieved.
label: sci.space

sentence: Then, the tilt maneuver (roll program) orients the vehicle to a heads down attitude required to generate a negative q-alpha, which in turn alleviates structural loading.
label: sci.space

sentence: Other advantages with this attitude are performance gain, decreased abort maneuver complexity, improved S-band look angles, and crew view of the horizon.
label: sci.space

sentence: The tilt maneuver is also required to start gaining downrange velocity to achieve the main engine cutoff (MECO) target in second stage."
label: sci.space

sentence: This really is a good answer, but it's couched in NASA jargon.
label: sci.space

sentence: I'll try to interpret.
label: sci.space

sentence: We wait until the Shuttle clears the tower before rolling.
label: sci.space

sentence: Then, we roll the Shuttle around so that the angle of attack between the wind caused by passage through the atmosphere (the "relative wind") and the chord of the wings (the imaginary line between the leading edge and the trailing edge) is a slightly negative angle ("a negative q-alpha").
label: sci.space

sentence: This causes a little bit of "downward" force (toward the belly of the Orbiter, or the +Z direction) and this force "alleviates structural loading."
label: sci.space

sentence: We have to be careful about those wings -- they're about the most "delicate" part of the vehicle.
label: sci.space

sentence: The new attitude (after the roll) also allows us to carry more mass to orbit, or to achieve a higher orbit with the same mass, or to change the orbit to a higher or lower inclination than would be the case if we didn't roll ("performance gain").
label: sci.space

sentence: The new attitude allows the crew to fly a less complicated flight path if they had to execute one of the more dangerous abort maneuvers, the Return To Launch Site ("decreased abort maneuver complexity").
label: sci.space

sentence: The new attitude improves the ability for ground-based radio antennae to have a good line-of-sight signal with the S-band radio antennae on the Orbiter ("improved S-band look angles").
label: sci.space

sentence: The new attitude allows the crew to see the horizon, which is a helpful (but not mandatory) part of piloting any flying machine.
label: sci.space

sentence: The new attitude orients the Shuttle so that the body is more nearly parallel with the ground, and the nose to the east (usually).
label: sci.space

sentence: This allows the thrust from the engines to add velocity in the correct direction to eventually achieve orbit.
label: sci.space

sentence: Remember: velocity is a vector quantity made of both speed and direction.
label: sci.space

sentence: The Shuttle has to have a large horizontal component to its velocity and a very small vertical component to attain orbit.
label: sci.space

sentence: This all begs the question, "Why isn't the launch pad oriented to give this nice attitude to begin with?
label: sci.space

sentence: Why does the Shuttle need to roll to achieve that attitude?"
label: sci.space

sentence: The answer is that the pads were leftovers from the Apollo days.
label: sci.space

sentence: The Shuttle straddles two flame trenches -- one for the Solid Rocket Motor exhaust, one for the Space Shuttle Main Engine exhaust.
label: sci.space

sentence: (You can see the effects of this on any daytime launch.
label: sci.space

sentence: The SRM exhaust is dirty gray garbage, and the SSME exhaust is fluffy white steam.
label: sci.space

sentence: Watch for the difference between the "top" [Orbiter side] and the "bottom" [External Tank side] of the stack.)
label: sci.space

sentence: The access tower and other support and service structure are all oriented basically the same way they were for the Saturn V's.
label: sci.space

sentence: (A side note: the Saturn V's also had a roll program.
label: sci.space

sentence: Don't ask me why -- I'm a Shuttle guy.)
label: sci.space

sentence: I checked with a buddy in Ascent Dynamics.
label: sci.space

sentence: He added that the "roll maneuver" is really a maneuver in all three axes: roll, pitch and yaw.
label: sci.space

sentence: The roll component of that maneuver is performed for the reasons stated.
label: sci.space

sentence: The pitch component controls loading on the wings by keeping the angle of attack (q-alpha) within a tight tolerance.
label: sci.space

sentence: The yaw component is used to determine the orbital inclination.
label: sci.space

sentence: The total maneuver is really expressed as a "quaternion," a grad-level-math concept for combining all three rotation matrices in one four-element array.

sentence: Okay, I guess its time for a quick explanation of Mac sound.
label: comp.sys.mac.hardware

sentence: The original documentation for the sound hardware (IM-3) documents how to make sound by directly accessing hardware.
label: comp.sys.mac.hardware

sentence: Basically, you jam values into all the even bytes from SoundBase to SoundBase+0x170.
label: comp.sys.mac.hardware

sentence: This was because of how the Mac 128 (and some later machines) generated sound was by scanning this block and D/Aing every even byte (the odd bytes went to the floppy on some machines).
label: comp.sys.mac.hardware

sentence: When the Mac II (and Apple Sound Chip) was invented, it was designed to generate stereo sound.
label: comp.sys.mac.hardware

sentence: It was also designed to be compatible with we had once documented.
label: comp.sys.mac.hardware

sentence: So storing bytes at the even values at SoundBase meant "I want to play a mono sound"
label: comp.sys.mac.hardware

sentence: and so it was emulated.
label: comp.sys.mac.hardware

sentence: But Apple had since retracted the documentation on SoundBase and decided not to document the lowest layers of sound generation.
label: comp.sys.mac.hardware

sentence: So apple never explained where to stuff bytes if you want to make stereo sound.
label: comp.sys.mac.hardware

sentence: Enter game developers.
label: comp.sys.mac.hardware

sentence: The sound driver and current sound manager are inconveniently lame for making games.
label: comp.sys.mac.hardware

sentence: Furthermore, people who port from the IBM don't want to learn more APIs.
label: comp.sys.mac.hardware

sentence: So, it has become popular for game writers to write to SoundBase to make sound, since it is very easy.
label: comp.sys.mac.hardware

sentence: Since the Mac II, Apple has had many different kind of sound output hardware, and the only things they have in common are that writing to SoundBase works.
label: comp.sys.mac.hardware

sentence: On some hardware, (Mac II, IIx, IIcx, IIci at least), writing to SoundBase gets you mono sound through both speakers.
label: comp.sys.mac.hardware

sentence: On some Macs (Quadra 700/900/950 at least) writing to SoundBase gets you mono sound on the left channel only.
label: comp.sys.mac.hardware

sentence: Both are technically correct interpretations of the original specification, but one is obviously preferable for asthetic reasons.
label: comp.sys.mac.hardware

sentence: If developers use the Sound Manager (and I think the sound driver), mono sounds will (typically) come out through both channels regardless of the underlying hardware.
label: comp.sys.mac.hardware

sentence: It is possible to specify you want to generate left channel only, but no one does.
label: comp.sys.mac.hardware

sentence: If developers write to sound base, their games will only come out the left on some games.
label: comp.sys.mac.hardware

sentence: The other problem, the original Mac II only producing left channel sound on the internal speaker, while the SE/30 mixes both, isn't really related.
label: comp.sys.mac.hardware

sentence: It is due to the post generation amplification used on the motherboards of the different machines.
label: comp.sys.mac.hardware

sentence: It only affects true stereo sounds.
label: comp.sys.mac.hardware

sentence: Sounds which are actually mono, will play on the internal speaker, no matter what machine you have.
label: comp.sys.mac.hardware

sentence: Other games you list: and also PGA Tour Golf Civilization (and more, these are again only from personal experience)

sentence: Use the sound manager and work fine on all machines.

sentence: Procedure 1) Insert usual disclaimer and antistatic warnings here.

sentence: 2) Remove the top lid of the machine.

label: comp.sys.mac.hardware

sentence: Unplug ALL cables, wall and monitor power supply cords from the back of the mac.
label: comp.sys.mac.hardware

sentence: 3) Remove the power supply by pulling the plastic interlocking tab on the tower forward and simultaneously pulling the power supply straight up.
label: comp.sys.mac.hardware

sentence: The tab is a piece of plastic from the left posterior aspect of the tower which extends downward to hook on to the power supply.
label: comp.sys.mac.hardware

sentence: You may also feel a horseshoe shaped piece at the right portion of the power supply.
label: comp.sys.mac.hardware

sentence: Leave that alone.
label: comp.sys.mac.hardware

sentence: The plastic tab from the tower is all you need release.
label: comp.sys.mac.hardware

sentence: 4) Look at the rear of the tower assembly.
label: comp.sys.mac.hardware

sentence: You will see the flat ribbon SCSI connector to the hard drive, a power cable and a flat ribbon cable leading to the floppy drive.
label: comp.sys.mac.hardware

sentence: Disconnect all these from the motherboard.
label: comp.sys.mac.hardware

sentence: The hard drive power cable connector has a tab which must be squeezed to release it.
label: comp.sys.mac.hardware

sentence: 5) Unplug the drive activity LED from its clear plastic mount 6)
label: comp.sys.mac.hardware

sentence: Look down the posterior, cylindrical section of the plastic tower.
label: comp.sys.mac.hardware

sentence: A phillips head screw is at the base.
label: comp.sys.mac.hardware

sentence: Remove it, taking care not to drop it into the case.
label: comp.sys.mac.hardware

sentence: A bit of gummy glue on your screwdriver is helpful here.
label: comp.sys.mac.hardware

sentence: 7) Remove the tower assembly by pulling medially the plastic tab on the right side of the tower.
label: comp.sys.mac.hardware

sentence: This tab prevents the tower from sliding posteriorly.
label: comp.sys.mac.hardware

sentence: Slide the entire tower assembly 1 cm posteriorly then lift the tower assembly straight up and out of the case.
label: comp.sys.mac.hardware

sentence: 8) Remove the interrupt switch assembly.
label: comp.sys.mac.hardware

sentence: It is a strangely shaped plastic device at the left, front edge of the motherboard.
label: comp.sys.mac.hardware

sentence: Pull the middle, rear plastic prong up and forward.
label: comp.sys.mac.hardware

sentence: The entire device will release.
label: comp.sys.mac.hardware

sentence: 9) Unplug the speaker cable.
label: comp.sys.mac.hardware

sentence: Squeeze the plastic tab on the speaker to free it then swing it backwards to free it from the case.
label: comp.sys.mac.hardware

sentence: 10) Remove the motherboard form the case.
label: comp.sys.mac.hardware

sentence: Lift the front right corner of the motherboard about 1 mm.
label: comp.sys.mac.hardware

sentence: This allows it to clear the clear plastic Power light guide.
label: comp.sys.mac.hardware

sentence: Slide the motherboard forward about 1 cm.
label: comp.sys.mac.hardware

sentence: The motherboard then comes directly out.
label: comp.sys.mac.hardware

sentence: 11) Locate the 50 mhz clock crystal.
label: comp.sys.mac.hardware

sentence: It is a small metal box near the CPU chip.
label: comp.sys.mac.hardware

sentence: Note and remember its orientation.
label: comp.sys.mac.hardware

sentence: The new clock oscillators must be aligned with pin 1 in the same orientation.
label: comp.sys.mac.hardware

sentence: Very carefully desolder and remove the old clock oscillator.
label: comp.sys.mac.hardware

sentence: Some of the pins may be bent over.
label: comp.sys.mac.hardware

sentence: Simply desolder then unbend them.
label: comp.sys.mac.hardware

sentence: Be sure your desoldering iron is hot enough before heating the board.
label: comp.sys.mac.hardware

sentence: I used a suction desoldering iron to accomplish this task.
label: comp.sys.mac.hardware

sentence: This is NOT appropriate for a first soldering experience.
label: comp.sys.mac.hardware

sentence: The motherboard is a multi-layer design with very fine traces - easily damaged without proper care.
label: comp.sys.mac.hardware

sentence: Install your socket or socket pins where the old oscillator once was.
label: comp.sys.mac.hardware

sentence: 13) Put a 50 mhz clock oscillator into the new socket.
label: comp.sys.mac.hardware

sentence: This may come of inside the socket and cause corrosion problems later.

sentence: I suggest using a new 50 mhz clock.

label: comp.sys.mac.hardware

sentence: Reinsert the motherboard and slide it into place.
label: comp.sys.mac.hardware

sentence: Plug the speaker wire back into the motherboard.
label: comp.sys.mac.hardware

sentence: Once fully down, slide the tower assembly anteriorly until it clicks into place.

sentence: ======================== The following are the main purposes this group shall achieve: i)
label: soc.religion.christian

sentence: To highlight the common beliefs of all major religions and philosophical traditions as they relate to Ahmadiyya Muslim Community. ii)
label: soc.religion.christian

sentence: To discuss the doctrines, origin and teachings the Ahmad- iyya Muslim Community, a dynamic world-wide movement.
label: soc.religion.christian

sentence: iii)
label: soc.religion.christian

sentence: To expound Islamic teachings and beliefs in the Holy Quran and Islamic traditions from the Ahmadiyya Islamic perspective.
label: soc.religion.christian

sentence: iv) To emphasize and discuss the similarities between Ahmadi Muslims and followers of other religions of the world and to explore how understanding and respect for each other's faith can be brought about to eliminate religious intol- erance and malice among people of all religious and phil- osophical traditions.
label: soc.religion.christian

sentence: v) To look into the origin and teachings of all religions in general and of Islam and Ahmadiyya Muslim Movement in par- ticular, and to use the commonality of origin to foster better understanding among Ahmadi Muslims and other people and to promote an acceptance of universality of fundamental rights to the freedom of conscience.
label: soc.religion.christian

sentence: vi)
label: soc.religion.christian

sentence: To point out current world problems and suggest solutions to these problems, as offered by different religions and systems of ethical philosophies. vii)
label: soc.religion.christian

sentence: To investigate the implications of science on religion with particular emphasis on the Ahmadi Muslim perspective, but with openness to dialogue with people of all religions and philosophical traditions with reasoned positions as to the relationship between religion and empirical science, logic, and scientific ethics.
label: soc.religion.christian

sentence: viii)
label: soc.religion.christian

sentence: To exchange important news and views about the Ahmadiyya Muslim Community and of other religions.
label: soc.religion.christian

sentence: ix)
label: soc.religion.christian

sentence: To add diversity to the existing religious newsgroups pre- sent on Usenet in the interest of promoting a forum for decorous dialogue.
label: soc.religion.christian

sentence: x)
label: soc.religion.christian

sentence: To inquire why religious persecution is on the rise in the world and suggest solutions to remedy the ever deterior- ating situation in the world in general and in the Islamic world in particular.
label: soc.religion.christian

sentence: xi)
label: soc.religion.christian

sentence: To commemorate the contributions to humanity, society and world peace made by the founders and followers of all religions in general and by the International Ahmadiyya Muslim community in particular.
label: soc.religion.christian

Rana <rana@rintintin.colorado.edu> Co-Moderator: Dr. Tahir Ijaz <ijaz@ccu.umanitoba.ca> A BRIEF DESCRIPTION ABOUT AHMADIYYA ISLAM: =============================================== The Ahmadiyya Movement in Islam, an international organi- sation, was found in 1889 in Qadian, India.
label: soc.religion.christian

sentence: The founder of this movement, Hazrat Mirza Ghulam Ahmad (1835-1908), was proclaimed to be the Promised Reformer of this age as foretold in the Scriptures of almost all major religions of the world.
label: soc.religion.christian

sentence: He claimed to be the fulfillment of the long awaited second comming of Jesus Christ (metaphorically), the Muslim Mahdi, and the Promised Messiah.
label: soc.religion.christian

sentence: The claims of Hazrat Ahmad raised storms of hostility and extreme opposition, which are often witnessed in the history of divine reformers.
label: soc.religion.christian

sentence: Even today this sect is being persecuted especial- ly in some of the Muslim regimes.
label: soc.religion.christian

sentence: The right of Ahmadi Muslims to openly practice their religion and to define themselves as Muslims has been severely restricted in many Muslim Countries.
label: soc.religion.christian

sentence: The United Nations, human rights organizations such as Amnesty International and top leaderships of some countries have voiced their concerns against this denial of basic human and civil liberaties to the members of this movement, but so far to no avail.
label: soc.religion.christian

sentence: Despite the opposition and persecution, the movement cont- inues to grow with a current membership of millions from around the world in over 130 countries, who come from diverse ethnic and cul- tural backgrounds.
label: soc.religion.christian

sentence: The movement is devoted to world peace and strives towards developing a better understanding of all religions.
label: soc.religion.christian

sentence: Ahmadi Muslims have always been opposed to all forms of violence, bigotry, reli- gious intolerance and fundamentalism.
label: soc.religion.christian

sentence: Among its many philanthropic activities, the sect has es- tablished a network of hundreds of schools, hospitals, and clinics in many third world countries.
label: soc.religion.christian

sentence: These institutions are staffed by volunteer professionals and are fully financed by the movement's internal resources.
label: soc.religion.christian

sentence: The movement stresses the importance of educa- tion and leadership.
label: soc.religion.christian

sentence: Its members have included a high number of professionals as well as world class individuals.
label: soc.religion.christian

sentence: The Ahmadiyya mission is to bring about a universal moral reform, establish peace and justice, and to unite mankind under one universal brotherhood.

sentence: This is significant, for if the term is so derived, it clearly refutes Boswell's claim that the first half of the word (arseno-) denotes not the object but the gender of the second half (-koitai).

sentence: The LXX must mean "a male who sleeps with a male," making arseno- the object.

sentence: Wright also faults Boswell's claims regarding linguistic features of the term, including suggested parallels (129).
